47.11: 'But evil shall come upon you, which you will not know how to conjure away; disaster shall fall on you, which you will be powerless to avert; and ruin shall come upon you suddenly, which you never saw coming' — the triple disaster announcement: (1) 'vetavo aleikh ra'ah lo ted'i shacharah = and evil will come upon you, you will not know its dawn/how to avert it' — the ra'ah (evil/disaster) coming without warning or means of avoidance; (2) 'vetippol aleikh hovah lo tukhal kapparoh = and disaster will fall on you, you will not be able to atone for/avert it' — the kapparoh (atonement/covering = the ritual means of covering/removing guilt or danger; Yom Kippur = the Day of Atonement). Babylon's own atonement-rituals will be powerless against YHWH's judgment; (3) 'vetavo aleikh pit'om shoah asher lo-tede'i = and ruin will come upon you suddenly, a desolation you did not know' — the shoah (sudden ruin/disaster; the same root as the Holocaust's name in Hebrew) coming without warning. Cyrus's conquest of Babylon in 539 BCE was indeed remarkably sudden and unexpected.
47.12: 'Stand by your enchantments and your many sorceries, which you have practiced since your youth; perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you can inspire fear' — the divine sarcasm inviting Babylon to try its magical arts: 'imdiy-na beverakhayikh uvrov kheshafayikh asher yaga'at mine'urayikh = stand now in your enchantments and your many sorceries in which you have labored since your youth.' The me'urayikh (since your youth = Babylon's magical arts were ancient, developed over centuries of Mesopotamian civilization going back to Sumer). 'Perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you can inspire fear' (ulai tukeli ho'il ulai ta'arotsi = perhaps you can prevail; perhaps you will inspire awe/fear) — the sarcastic 'perhaps' (ulai): in the face of YHWH's judgment, the most the magical arts can offer is a 'perhaps.' The entire history of Babylonian divination — the omen series, the astrological texts, the incantation rituals — is dismissed as offering only tentative and ineffectual results against divine action.
47.13: 'You are worn out by all your advisers; let them stand up and save you — those who divide the sky, who gaze at the stars, who at each new moon predict what will happen to you' — the exhaustion of Babylonian knowledge-systems: 'nile'it berov atsotayikh = you are wearied by the multitude of your counselors.' Three classes of Babylonian religious specialists: (1) 'chotevei shamayim = those who divide/cut the sky' = the astrologers who divided the heavens into zones for omen-interpretation (the Babylonian system divided the sky into three 'roads': the Road of Anu, the Road of Enlil, and the Road of Ea); (2) 'hachozim bakokahvim = those who gaze at the stars' = the astronomical observers who tracked planetary and stellar movements (the Babylonian astronomical cuneiform texts represent the most sophisticated pre-modern astronomy, with accurate records of planetary cycles, eclipses, etc.); (3) 'meyodi'im lechadashim = those who make known by months' = the monthly prognosticators who issued monthly horoscope-style predictions (the Babylonian Almanacs and Goal-Year texts). All three classes of expert are invited to save Babylon — knowing the outcome in advance.
47.14: 'Look — they are like straw; the fire devours them; they cannot save themselves from the power of the flame. There is no coal for warmth, no fire to sit by' — the judgment by fire: 'hineh hayu kaqash esh serapatam = see, they are like straw; fire has burned them.' The astrologers and diviners compared to qash (straw/chaff = the light, inflammable residue of the threshing floor, the same chaff that the wind blows away in 40:24; 41:16). 'They cannot save themselves from the power of the flame' (lo-yatsillu et-nafsham miyad lehabah = they cannot deliver their souls from the hand of the flame). The irony: the specialists who were supposed to 'save' Babylon from disaster cannot save themselves. 'There is no coal for warmth, no fire to sit by' (ein-gachelet lehamam ur lagshevet legandeha = there is no coal to warm, no fire to sit before it) — the fire of judgment produces no warmth or benefit, only destruction. Contrast with the idol-maker's fire in 44:16 ('Aha, I am warm!').
47.15: 'Such are those you have dealt with, who have been your trading partners since your youth; they each go off in their own direction; there is no one to save you' — the dissolution of Babylon's commercial network: 'ken hayu lakh asher yaga'at socharayikh mine'urayikh = such are those to you for whom you have labored, your traders from your youth.' The trading partners (sokherayikh = your merchants/traders) who have benefited from Babylon's commercial empire 'since your youth' (mine'urayikh = from your youth = the centuries of Babylonian commercial dominance). 'They each go off in their own direction' (ish ledarkho ta'u = each wanders to his own path) — the commercial abandonment: when Babylon falls, the trading partners scatter, each pursuing their own survival. 'There is no one to save you' (en moshi'ekh = there is no one saving you) — the final verdict: the trading partners, the astrologers, the enchanters — all fail to save. Only YHWH is moshi'a (savior = 43:11; 45:21), and he is judging Babylon, not protecting it. Rev 18:9-19 expands this commercial-abandonment motif for the eschatological Babylon.

48.1: The oracle opens with a formal summons addressed to the house of Jacob — those who swear by the name of YHWH but not in truth or righteousness. Swearing by a deity was a binding social act in the ANE that invoked divine witness and staked one's honor-shame reputation on truthfulness. Swearing by YHWH without sincerity was a category violation: using the covenant God as guarantor of hollow speech.
48.2: They call themselves of the holy city and lean on the God of Israel. The title holy city for Jerusalem reflects the ANE concept of the divine resident conferring sanctity on the urban space. Leaning on YHWH while refusing genuine loyalty echoes suzerainty treaty violations — the vassal invokes the great king's name while conducting independent policy.
48.3: YHWH declares the former things announced from of old. In the ancient world, a deity's ability to foretell events before they happened was proof of sovereignty over time — a polemic against Babylonian divination systems that claimed to read the future through omens. YHWH's pre-announcements, now fulfilled, establish his credibility.
48.4: The reason for prior announcement is stated bluntly: because I knew you are obstinate (literally iron-neck) and your forehead bronze. Iron-necked cattle resist the yoke; bronze forehead imagery derives from ANE metallurgy — the hardest, most unyielding metal. These are honor-shame terms: the people's stubbornness is shameful intransigence toward their covenant lord.
48.5: YHWH told them future events before they arrived so they could not credit idols — my idol did this, my carved image commanded it. This is the core anti-idol polemic of Deutero-Isaiah: Babylonian religion attributed historical outcomes to Marduk, Nebo, and the divine assembly. YHWH's pre-announcement forecloses that attribution.
48.6: You have heard — see it all. Will you not declare it? The prophetic tradition expected the recipient of divine disclosure to become a witness. Now YHWH announces new things — hidden things not known before. Second Isaiah distinguishes between former prophecies (now verifiable) and genuinely new acts that no prior tradition anticipated.
48.7: The new things are created now, not from of old — not something Jacob heard before, lest they say I knew it. ANE prophets sometimes retrodicted — framing past events as predictions. YHWH explicitly denies this: this is genuinely new revelation, not a recycled oracle.
48.8: You neither heard nor knew — your ear was not opened from of old. The closed ear is a covenant-failure image. YHWH knew Jacob would deal treacherously and was called a rebel from birth. Prenatal designation appears in ancient literature to emphasize divine foreknowledge: the character was set at origin, not formed by circumstance.
48.9: For my own name's sake I restrain my anger. The phrase lema'ani (for my sake) is the theological fulcrum: divine patience is not primarily humanitarian but oriented to YHWH's reputation among nations. Restraining wrath is itself an honor-preserving act — punishing Israel too completely would invite the taunt that YHWH abandoned or failed his people.
48.10: Behold, I have refined you — but not as silver; I have tested you in the furnace of affliction. This is a crucial departure from standard smelting metaphor: the exile was not a silver-refining that produced pure metal. The furnace of affliction (Egypt, Babylon) tested loyalty but did not purify Israel into religious perfection. Honest historiography about covenant failure.
48.11: For my own sake I do it — how should my name be profaned? The double lema'ani (for my sake, v. 9 and v. 11) frames the unit. YHWH's motivation for salvation is self-referential: his glory cannot be shared with idols. Ezekiel 36 parallels this logic: restoration is for YHWH's name, which Israel had profaned among the nations.
48.12: Listen to me, O Jacob, Israel whom I called: I am he; I am the first and also the last. The formula I am he (ani hu) and first-and-last framing is YHWH's self-designation as sole sovereign over history's entire arc. Babylonian creation mythology attributed cosmic order to Marduk's victory over Tiamat; YHWH claims primacy before any such battle.
48.13: My hand laid the foundation of the earth and my right hand spread out the heavens. Calling heaven and earth together was a formal invocation — witnesses appear when summoned. Creator status grounds everything that follows: one who made the cosmic order has authority to undo Babylonian power.
48.14: Assemble and hear: who among the idols has declared these things? YHWH has loved Cyrus — he will carry out YHWH's purpose against Babylon. Cyrus ben Cambyses II conquered Babylon in 539 BCE, entering the city as a liberator. That a foreign king would be YHWH's instrument against Israel's captor had no precedent in Israelite theology.
48.15: I, even I, have spoken — yes, I have called him, brought him, and he will succeed. The emphatic first-person (ani ani) mirrors the self-designation of powerful ANE rulers in royal inscriptions. YHWH's declaration of Cyrus's success before Cyrus has acted functions like a divine decree tablet — the outcome is sealed.
48.16: Draw near and hear this: from the beginning I have not spoken in secret — from the time it was, I was there. The mysterious close of the verse — and now the Lord YHWH has sent me and his Spirit — introduces a speaker who is neither YHWH alone nor the prophet: a figure sent by YHWH together with his Spirit. This has been read in Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity as a Trinitarian trace.
48.17: Thus says YHWH your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: I am YHWH your God who teaches you to profit, who leads you in the way you should go. The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) imagery invokes the legal institution where a near kinsman buys back lost land or redeems an enslaved relative. YHWH as Israel's go'el grounds the restoration in family-covenant obligation.
48.18: If only you had paid attention to my commandments — your peace would have been like a river and your righteousness like waves of the sea. The counterfactual lamentation uses two of the most abundant natural images in a land-constrained landscape: the Jordan river and the Mediterranean's endless waves. Shalom as river-current captures the idea of inexhaustible, self-renewing wellbeing.
48.19: Your offspring would have been like the sand and your descendants like its grains — their name would not be cut off or destroyed. Sand-numerous offspring is Abrahamic covenant language (Gen 22:17). The counterfactual tragically inverts the promise: covenant faithfulness would have secured what disobedience forfeited.
48.20: Go out from Babylon! Flee from the Chaldeans! With a shout of joy, declare this, proclaim it — YHWH has redeemed his servant Jacob. The imperative surge launches the new-Exodus announcement. Babylon is the new Egypt; the Chaldeans are the new Pharaoh. The shout to the ends of the earth echoes the Song of the Sea (Exod 15) — cosmic testimony to the exodus event.
48.21: They did not thirst when he led them through the deserts — he made water flow for them from the rock, splitting the rock and the water gushed out. The wilderness-water miracle (Exod 17; Num 20) is explicitly invoked as template for the new Exodus. The return from Babylon will recapitulate the wilderness provision — YHWH's faithfulness has structural continuity across eras.
48.22: There is no peace for the wicked, says YHWH. The refrain appears again at 57:21, bracketing the central section of Second Isaiah. It distinguishes the restored community from those who refuse the covenant. In ANE treaty contexts, shalom was the condition of covenantal security; its absence signals exclusion from the covenant relationship.

49.1: The Servant calls to the coastlands and distant peoples to listen — YHWH called him from the womb and named him before birth. Prenatal divine commissioning is a mark of prophetic legitimacy (Jer 1:5) but here the scale exceeds any individual prophet: the audience is the entire known world, from Mediterranean coastlands to eastern nations. This is the Second Servant Song (vv. 1–6).
49.2: He made my mouth like a sharp sword and hid me in the shadow of his hand — a polished arrow concealed in his quiver. The hidden weapon imagery reflects ANE military practice of concealing elite troops until the strategic moment. The Servant's word is both sword and precision arrow — instruments of conquest without conventional warfare.
49.3: He said to me, You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified. The Servant is named Israel — a point of intense interpretive debate in Second Temple Judaism and Christianity. Whether corporate Israel or an individual representative who embodies Israel's vocation, the identification grounds the Servant's mission in covenant identity.
49.4: But I said, I have labored in vain — I have spent my strength for nothing and vanity, yet my cause is with YHWH and my reward with my God. The lament of apparent futility (tohu, the same word as Gen 1:2 formless void) paradoxically trusts YHWH with the outcome. This mirrors the psalms of lament where the petitioner brings honest failure to the covenant lord.
49.5: YHWH formed me from the womb to be his servant — to bring Jacob back to him, and Israel would be gathered to him. The Servant's commission begins as a restoration task for ethnic Israel, consistent with the standard prophetic call. The honor is not diminished by apparent failure; YHWH is his strength.
49.6: It is too small a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob — I will make you a light for the nations so that my salvation may reach the ends of the earth. The commission's expansion from Israel-restoration to universal light-for-nations (goyim) is the theological breakthrough. The Servant's scope exceeds any prior prophetic role. Paul quotes this verse (Acts 13:47) as scriptural basis for Gentile mission.
49.7: Thus says YHWH, the Redeemer of Israel, his Holy One, to one deeply despised, abhorred by the nation, the servant of rulers: Kings shall see and arise, princes and they shall prostrate themselves. The reversal pattern — abject humiliation followed by royal prostration — is standard ANE royal theology inverted. It is the shamed servant, not the powerful ruler, who commands international honor.
49.8: In a time of favor I have answered you — in a day of salvation I have helped you. Paul quotes this verse directly in 2 Corinthians 6:2, applying it to the present eschatological moment. The phrase time of favor (et ratzon) is a technical term for the acceptable, appointed period when YHWH's disposition toward the petitioner is favorable — a window to be seized.
49.9: Saying to the prisoners, Come out — to those in darkness, Show yourselves. They shall feed along the ways and on all bare heights shall be their pasture. The prisoner-release announcement echoes the Jubilee year (Lev 25), when debt-slaves were freed. The pastoral imagery (feeding along roads) reimagines the return from exile as an Edenic provision — the wilderness itself becomes a feeding ground.
49.10: They shall not hunger or thirst, neither scorching wind nor sun shall strike them — for he who has compassion on them will lead them and guide them to springs of water. The wilderness-provision motif continues. The compassion-shepherd who leads to water springs inverts the death-by-exposure fate of refugees crossing desert terrain — the threat becomes the site of miraculous provision.
49.11: I will make all my mountains a road and my highways shall be raised up. The cosmic road-building project recapitulates the new-Exodus highway of Isaiah 40:3–4 — valleys exalted, mountains lowered. In ANE royal ideology, road-building demonstrated imperial power; YHWH surpasses all empires by reshaping geography itself.
49.12: Behold, these shall come from afar — from the north and from the west and from the land of Sinim. The ingathering from Sinim (possibly Aswan/Egypt or a far eastern region) extends the return beyond Babylon to the entire diaspora. The universal ingathering of scattered Israel from all compass points is a restored-Eden vision.
49.13: Sing for joy, O heavens, and exult, O earth — break forth into singing, O mountains! For YHWH has comforted his people and will have compassion on his afflicted. The cosmic chorus (heaven, earth, mountains) is the creation's response to covenant renewal. ANE hymns regularly called on the natural order to celebrate divine victories; here creation celebrates Israel's redemption.
49.14: But Zion said, YHWH has forsaken me — my Lord has forgotten me. The lament of abandonment uses the strongest possible covenant-failure language: forsaken (azav) and forgotten (shakach) are the covenant curses of Deuteronomy. Zion personified voices the communal experience of exile as divine rejection.
49.15: Can a woman forget her nursing child — have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you. The mother-nursing-infant image is the most intimate possible attachment in the ANE household. The rhetorical question sets the emotional stakes: even if the strongest biological bond could break, YHWH's memory of Zion cannot.
49.16: Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands — your walls are continually before me. Engraving names on palms was not a common ANE practice, making the image startling: YHWH carries Jerusalem's name as a permanent bodily inscription. The walls of the destroyed city are always visible to him — he has not looked away.
49.17: Your builders come quickly — your destroyers and those who laid you waste go out from you. The reversal of Babylon's demolition of Jerusalem: the destroyers depart and builders rush in. This is the concrete logistics of return-and-rebuild, framed as YHWH's direct action.
49.18: Lift up your eyes and see — they all gather and come to you. As I live, declares YHWH, you shall put them all on as an ornament and bind them on as a bride does. The returning children are jewelry and bridal ornament — a striking image of communal identity restored. The oath formula As I live (chai ani) is a divine self-invocation, the strongest possible assurance in ANE discourse.
49.19: Your waste and your desolate places and your devastated land — surely now you will be too narrow for your inhabitants, and those who swallowed you will be far away. Overpopulation after desolation is the sign of blessing (cf. Gen 26:22). The repopulation astonishment theme runs through the restoration oracles: more children than the land can hold.
49.20: The children born in the time of your bereavement will yet say in your hearing, The place is too narrow for me — give me a place to dwell in. The children of exile — born during national death — outlive the catastrophe and demand living space. The voice of the next generation claiming territory is the sound of covenant restoration.
49.21: Then you will say in your heart, Who has borne me these? I was bereaved and barren, exiled and put away — who brought these up? I was left alone; from where have these come? The astonishment-question pattern appears in ANE royal annals when a king's territory expands beyond expectation. Zion's bewilderment at her own restoration is the appropriate response to divine surprise.
49.22: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I will lift up my hand to the nations and raise my signal to the peoples — they shall bring your sons in their arms and your daughters shall be carried on their shoulders. Lifting the hand was the ANE gesture of oath-swearing and military signal. Nations become the transport fleet for Israel's return — reversal of the deportation that brought exiles to Babylon.
49.23: Kings shall be your foster fathers and their queens your nursing mothers — they shall bow down to you with their faces to the ground and lick the dust of your feet. The prostration-and-dust imagery is drawn from ANE royal submission iconography. Vassal kings prostrated to the great king and kissed his feet; here that gesture is redirected to Israel by the very kings who once dominated her.
49.24: Can the prey be taken from the mighty man, or the captive of a tyrant be rescued? The rhetorical question invokes the logic of power: once a conqueror holds captives, no one challenges him. The expected answer is no — making YHWH's rescue all the more astonishing when the next verse overturns it.
49.25: For thus says YHWH: Even the captives of the mighty shall be taken and the prey of the tyrant delivered — for I will contend with those who contend with you, and I will save your children. YHWH as Israel's divine warrior enters the legal-military contest on Israel's behalf. The verb contend (rib) is both legal and military — a covenant lawsuit combined with battle.
49.26: I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh and they shall be drunk with their own blood as with wine — then all flesh shall know that I, YHWH, am your Savior and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob. The self-cannibalization image appears in ANE siege-warfare descriptions as the ultimate horror of defeat. Here the oppressors' destruction becomes the proof of YHWH's identity as Israel's kinsman-redeemer.

50.1: YHWH asks: where is your mother's certificate of divorce? Or which of my creditors have I sold you to? The divorce-certificate (sefer keritut) of Deuteronomy 24:1 was a legal document formally ending the marriage covenant. YHWH's rhetorical question denies that any such document exists: Israel's exile was not a formal covenant divorce but a disciplinary consequence of Israel's own sin.
50.2: Why was no one there when I came? When I called, why did no one answer? Is my hand too short to redeem? YHWH's coming with no one to answer inverts the covenant call-and-response pattern. The dried-up sea and rivers imagery alludes to the Exodus parting of the Red Sea — YHWH dried waters then and can do it again. His power has not diminished.
50.3: I clothe the heavens with darkness and make sackcloth their covering. Cosmic mourning-dress signals the day-of-the-LORD theophany pattern. Sackcloth is the garment of grief and penitence throughout the ANE; when YHWH clothes the sky in it, creation mourns in solidarity with covenant judgment.
50.4: The Lord YHWH has given me the tongue of those who are taught (limmudim) so that I may know how to sustain with a word the one who is weary — morning by morning he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught. The Third Servant Song opens (vv. 4–9). Limmudim (discipled ones) is the same root as Isaiah 8:16 — those formed in the prophetic school. Morning-by-morning awakening is the spiritual discipline of daily divine formation, contrasted with the people who would not listen (v. 2).
50.5: The Lord YHWH has opened my ear and I was not rebellious — I turned not backward. The Servant's open ear directly contrasts with Israel's closed ear of 48:8. Non-rebellion and non-retreat are the Servant's fundamental posture — complete submission to the one who sends, even before the mission's cost is revealed.
50.6: I gave my back to those who strike and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard — I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting. The three actions — flogging, beard-pulling, spitting — are each severe honor-shame violations in the ANE. Beard-pulling was a profound insult to masculine dignity. Spitting in the face was the ultimate ritual humiliation. The Servant accepts all three deliberately, without resistance. The Gospel accounts of Jesus's trial (Mark 14:65; Matt 26:67; John 18:22) explicitly fulfill this.
50.7: But the Lord YHWH helps me — therefore I have not been disgraced. Therefore I have set my face like a flint and I know I shall not be put to shame. The flint-face image connotes immovable resolve in the face of humiliation. Luke 9:51 echoes this in describing Jesus setting his face toward Jerusalem. The Servant's confidence is not self-generated but grounded in divine vindication — the honor-shame calculus is inverted.
50.8: He who vindicates me is near — who will contend with me? Let us stand up together. Who is my adversary? Let him come near to me. Legal language saturates the verse: vindicator (matzdiki), contend (yarib), adversary (ba'al mishpat). The Servant issues a courtroom challenge — assured of his vindicator's presence, he welcomes any accuser to make their case.
50.9: Behold, the Lord YHWH helps me — who will declare me guilty? They will all wear out like a garment; the moth will eat them. The moth-and-garment decay image appears in Job and Psalms as a figure for the fragility of the wicked. The Servant's accusers, however powerful in the short term, have the substance of fabric — they will disintegrate.
50.10: Who among you fears YHWH and obeys the voice of his servant? Let him who walks in darkness and has no light trust in the name of YHWH and rely on his God. Two communities are distinguished: those who fear YHWH walk in darkness without any experiential light — yet they trust. This is the pattern of faith without sight, the condition of the exile community that cannot yet see the promised return.
50.11: Behold, all you who kindle a fire — who equip yourselves with burning torches — walk by the light of your fire and by the torches you have lit. This you shall have from my hand: you shall lie down in maschit (a place of destruction/torment). The self-lit fire represents human-originated religious or political schemes — Babylonian astrology, Egyptian alliances, any alternative to trust in YHWH. Those who navigate by their own light receive their own light's outcome: destruction.

51.1: Listen to me, you who pursue righteousness — you who seek YHWH. Look to the rock from which you were hewn and to the quarry from which you were dug. The double imperative listen / look summons the discouraged remnant. Rock and quarry are the ancestral origin — the geological stability of Abraham's family as template for restoration from exile's rubble.
51.2: Look to Abraham your father and to Sarah who bore you — for he was but one when I called him, that I might bless him and multiply him. The one-to-many logic: Abraham was single when called; the covenant produced a nation. The exilic remnant, however small, stands in the same structural position as the one ancestor. Numerical weakness does not disqualify divine promise.
51.3: For YHWH will comfort Zion — he will comfort all her waste places. He will make her wilderness like Eden and her desert like the garden of YHWH — joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the voice of song. The wilderness-to-Eden reversal is the creation-restoration vision. Eden as the template for end-state restoration appears in Ezekiel's river vision (Ezek 47) and the Revelation's new Jerusalem. The wilderness between Babylon and Jerusalem becomes a garden.
51.4: Pay attention to me, my people — give ear to me, my nation. A law will go out from me and I will set my justice as a light to the peoples. The Torah-light going to the nations extends the Servant's light-for-goyim commission (49:6) to YHWH himself. ANE treaty law was the great king's gift to vassal peoples; here YHWH's cosmic instruction extends beyond Israel to all nations.
51.5: My righteousness is near — my salvation has gone out and my arms will judge the peoples. The coastlands hope in me and for my arm they wait. The divine warrior extends justice to the coastlands — the furthest reaches of the known world. Coastlands (iyim) recurs through Second Isaiah as the representative extremity of gentile geography.
51.6: Lift up your eyes to the heavens and look at the earth beneath — for the heavens vanish like smoke, the earth wears out like a garment, and its inhabitants die like gnats. But my salvation will be forever and my righteousness will never be dismayed. Cosmic transience contrasts with eternal salvation: the most permanent-seeming things (sky, earth) are temporary; YHWH's covenant fidelity alone is permanent. This grounds trust in the most enduring possible foundation.
51.7: Listen to me, you who know righteousness — the people in whose heart is my law. Fear not the reproach of man and be not dismayed at their revilings. The community addressed has internalized Torah (law in the heart anticipates Jer 31:33). Their enemy is not physical assault but social reproach — the ANE honor-shame pressure of ridicule and contempt from the surrounding culture.
51.8: For the moth will eat them like a garment and the worm will eat them like wool — but my righteousness will be forever and my salvation to all generations. The moth-and-worm decay image (also 50:9) frames the oppressors as perishable fabric. The covenant community endures beyond the empires that mock them.
51.9: Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of YHWH — awake as in days of old, the generations of long ago. Was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces and pierced the dragon? The double imperative awake, awake summons the divine warrior. Rahab here is not the Jericho innkeeper but the chaos-sea-monster of ANE cosmogony — cognate with Babylonian Tiamat, Ugaritic Lotan. YHWH's Exodus defeat of Egypt is reread through the ANE chaos-combat myth lens: he defeated both the historical empire and the cosmic dragon behind it.
51.10: Was it not you who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep — who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to pass over? The Exodus sea-parting is the central historical proof of YHWH's power over the chaos waters. The same power is now invoked for the new Exodus from Babylon — the redeemed will again cross what seemed impassable.
51.11: And the ransomed of YHWH shall return and come to Zion with singing — everlasting joy shall be upon their heads. They shall obtain gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing shall flee away. This verse is nearly identical to 35:10 — a deliberate intertextual echo that frames the entire intervening section (chs. 36–51) as the theological bridge between the two identical conclusions. The ransomed are the go'el-redeemed community.
51.12: I, I myself, am he who comforts you — who are you that you fear man who dies? The emphatic double first-person (anochi anochi) is the divine self-assertion formula. The rhetorical question inverts the honor-shame calculus: fearing a mortal human rather than the immortal creator is an epistemological failure. The human oppressor is categorically finite; YHWH is not.
51.13: You have forgotten YHWH your maker who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth — and you feared continually because of the wrath of the oppressor when he sets himself to destroy. Creator-amnesia generates tyrant-fear: forgetting who made heaven and earth leaves the oppressor seeming like the largest power in the field of vision.
51.14: He who is bowed down shall quickly be released — he shall not die and go down to the pit. His food shall not lack. The prisoner-release announcement is concrete: the exiled community bowed under Babylonian servitude will be released before death (not post-mortem) and will not starve in the pit. Pit (shachat) is the underworld destination of the dishonored dead.
51.15: For I am YHWH your God who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — YHWH of hosts is his name. The storm-sea imagery is both creation and covenant: YHWH controls the chaos waters. YHWH of hosts (YHWH tsvaot) is the divine warrior title — commander of the heavenly armies. Military and cosmic sovereignty converge.
51.16: I have put my words in your mouth and covered you in the shadow of my hand — establishing the heavens and laying the foundation of the earth and saying to Zion, You are my people. Words-in-mouth is the formal prophetic commissioning formula (Jer 1:9; Deut 18:18). YHWH's creative word that made the cosmos is the same word now placed in the Servant/prophet's mouth for the new creation.
51.17: Rouse yourself, rouse yourself, stand up, O Jerusalem — you who have drunk from the hand of YHWH the cup of his wrath. You have drunk to the dregs the bowl, the cup of staggering, and drained it. The cup-of-wrath metaphor is standard in ANE treaty-curse literature: the vassal who breaks covenant drinks the cup of divine judgment. Jerusalem has already drained it completely — the punishment is finished.
51.18: There is none to guide her among all the sons she has borne — there is none to take her by the hand among all the sons she has brought up. Leaderlessness is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy — the community lacks competent leadership as sign of divine withdrawal. Jerusalem is a mother abandoned by all her children, unable to navigate without a guide.
51.19: These two things have come upon you — who will console you? Devastation and destruction, famine and sword — who will comfort you? The four-fold disaster catalog (devastation, destruction, famine, sword) covers both the military siege and its aftermath. The rhetorical question invites YHWH's answer, which comes in v. 22: I am the one who contends for you.
51.20: Your sons have fainted — they lie at the head of every street like an antelope in a net. They are full of the wrath of YHWH, the rebuke of your God. The antelope-in-net image captures complete helplessness: a creature too exhausted or entrapped to move. The streets filled with collapsed bodies evokes the aftermath of the Babylonian siege of 586 BCE.
51.21: Therefore hear this, you who are afflicted — you who are drunk, but not with wine. The formula drunk but not with wine distinguishes divine-wrath intoxication from ordinary inebriation. The covenant-curse literature used drunkenness as metaphor for the disorientation of judgment; YHWH's people have experienced this sober horror.
51.22: Thus says your Lord, YHWH, your God who pleads the cause of his people: Behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering — the bowl of my wrath you shall drink no more. The divine reversal: the cup transferred from Israel's hand. YHWH as legal advocate (rib) intercedes for the people he had judged. The punishment is definitively over.
51.23: And I will put it into the hand of your tormentors who said to you, Bow down that we may pass over — and you made your back like the ground and like the street for them to pass over. The specific humiliation — human backs used as pavement — appears in ANE military victory reliefs and inscriptions depicting conquered peoples as literal footstools. The cup of wrath passes from Jerusalem to Babylon.

52.1: Awake, awake, put on your strength, O Zion — put on your beautiful garments, O Jerusalem the holy city. For there shall no more come into you the uncircumcised and the unclean. The double awake, awake mirrors 51:9 (arm of the LORD) and 51:17 (Jerusalem) — the third and climactic summons. The beautiful garments replace the exile's rags and the mourning dress of Babylon. Purity categories (uncircumcised, unclean) defined sacred space access throughout the ANE; the restored holy city will enforce covenant boundaries. Revelation 21:27 echoes this exclusion.
52.2: Shake yourself from the dust and arise — be seated, O Jerusalem. Loose the bonds from your neck, O captive daughter of Zion. Dust-sitting was the posture of public mourning in the ANE; rising from the dust is the gesture of restoration to dignity. The neck-bond is the literal yoke of servitude — a common iconographic symbol in Assyrian and Babylonian relief art showing captive peoples with rope-collars.
52.3: For thus says YHWH: You were sold for nothing and you shall be redeemed without money (chinnam). The technical term chinnam (for nothing, gratis) appears in both the selling and the redemption: neither transaction involved payment. Israel's exile was not a legitimate commercial transaction but an act of divine discipline; the return requires no ransom price, because YHWH owns the outcome.
52.4: For thus says the Lord YHWH: My people went down at the first into Egypt to sojourn there and the Assyrian oppressed them for nothing. The historical survey begins with Egypt (the first sojourn-and-oppression cycle) and adds Assyria (the second). The pattern of oppression-for-nothing recurs across Israel's history — each oppressor acted without legitimate claim.
52.5: Now therefore what have I here — declares YHWH — for my people are taken away for nothing? Their rulers wail, declares YHWH, and continually all the day my name is despised. YHWH's name being despised by the nations because of Israel's exile is the Ezekiel 36:20–22 logic precisely: the nations said, These are YHWH's people and yet they had to go out of his land — making YHWH appear impotent. Israel's restoration is motivated in part by divine reputation.
52.6: Therefore my people shall know my name — therefore in that day they shall know that it is I who speak; here am I. The repeated therefore drives the logic: name-despising → name-vindication → covenant knowledge. The self-presentation formula here am I (hineni) is the divine answer to a human call — YHWH responds to his own summons.
52.7: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news (mevasser) — who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness, who publishes salvation, who says to Zion, Your God reigns! The herald-feet image captures the desperate waiting of a city for battle news. Mevasser is the verbal root behind Greek euangelion (gospel). Your God reigns is the royal acclamation — the new king's enthronement announcement. Paul quotes this verse (Rom 10:15); Mark 1:15 (the kingdom of God is at hand) is its fulfillment announcement.
52.8: The voice of your watchmen — they lift up their voice, together they sing for joy. For eye to eye they see the return of YHWH to Zion. Watchmen on city walls were the first to see approaching messengers. Here all the watchmen simultaneously see YHWH's return — the divine warrior coming back to his city after the exile judgment. Eye-to-eye seeing is unmediated, certain perception — no ambiguity, no prophetic vision, direct sight.
52.9: Break forth together into singing, you waste places of Jerusalem — for YHWH has comforted his people; he has redeemed Jerusalem. The ruined places of the city become the singing choir. The comfort (nicham) and redemption (ga'al) announce that both the pastoral and the legal-kinsman dimensions of YHWH's action are complete.
52.10: YHWH has bared his holy arm before the eyes of all the nations and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God. Baring the arm is the warrior's preparation-for-combat gesture — rolling up the sleeve to strike. The holy arm is visible to all nations simultaneously: the new Exodus is a universal, public event, not a private tribal transaction.
52.11: Depart, depart, go out from there — touch no unclean thing. Go out from the midst of her and be pure, you who bear the vessels of YHWH. The purity-departure call applies specifically to the temple-vessel carriers — the Levitical personnel responsible for the sacred equipment Nebuchadnezzar took to Babylon (2 Kgs 25:13–17). The return of the vessels (fulfilled under Cyrus, Ezra 1:7–11) is a sacral procession requiring ritual purity. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 6:17 as the basis for the community's separation from idolatry.
52.12: For you shall not go out in haste and you shall not go in flight — for YHWH will go before you and the God of Israel will be your rear guard. The deliberate, unhurried nature of the new Exodus contrasts with the Passover night exodus from Egypt (be-chipazon — in haste, Exod 12:11; Deut 16:3). YHWH as vanguard and rear guard forms the divine sandwich that protects the march — both military and liturgical imagery.
52.13: Behold, my servant shall act wisely — he shall be high and lifted up and shall be exalted. The Fourth Servant Song opens (52:13–53:12). Three ascending Hebrew verbs: yaskil (prosper/act wisely), yarum (be high), nissa (be lifted up), gav'ah (be exalted very high). Isaiah 6:1 uses the same verb pair (ram ve-nissa) to describe YHWH on his throne — the Servant shares the divine elevation vocabulary.
52.14: As many were appalled at him — his appearance was so marred, beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind. The appalled response (shamemu) is the reaction to something so horrible it silences observers. The marring language is extreme: the Servant's disfigurement exceeds the human form entirely — a body destroyed beyond recognition. This is read in Christian tradition as the crucifixion's physical reality.
52.15: So he shall startle many nations — kings shall shut their mouths because of him. For that which has not been told them they see and that which they have not heard they understand. The verb yazzeh (startle/sprinkle) is debated: it can mean to cause nations to leap/start in shock, or it can be the priestly sprinkling term used in ritual purification (Lev 4:6; 16:14–19). If the latter, the Servant performs a cosmic purification of many nations — a priestly act on an international scale. Kings' silenced mouths is the courtroom gesture of speechlessness before an overwhelming divine revelation.

54.1: Sing for joy, barren woman who has never given birth — for the children of the desolate woman will outnumber the children of the married woman. The barren-woman-who-surpasses pattern runs through Israel's foundational narratives: Sarah (Gen 21:2), Rachel (Gen 30:24), Hannah (1 Sam 1:20). The desolate one producing more children than the one with a husband is an honor-shame inversion: barrenness was the deepest social shame for an ANE woman, and numerical progeny the highest status marker. The desolate one is Zion in exile; the married one is Babylon at the height of her power.
54.2: Enlarge the space of your tent — stretch out the curtains of your dwelling; lengthen your ropes and drive your tent pegs deeper. Nomadic tent expansion was the immediate consequence of family growth: more children meant more living space, requiring longer guy-ropes staked farther out and more tent panels stitched together. The image combines the domestic intimacy of the Bedouin household with the expansionary logic of covenant blessing. The instruction to enlarge before the children arrive is a command to act in anticipatory faith.
54.3: For you will spread out to the right and to the left — your descendants will reclaim nations and resettle the ruined cities. Spreading right and left was the directional formula for territorial expansion in ANE treaties (cf. Gen 28:14). Reclaiming nations suggests the returned exiles will not merely inhabit Judah but extend influence to surrounding territories. Resettling ruined cities describes the literal condition of post-587 BCE Judah: the Babylonian campaign left most cities uninhabitable. The oracle promises demographic reversal.
54.4: Do not be afraid — you will not be put to shame. You will forget the disgrace of your youth and the reproach of your widowhood you will no longer remember. The shame of youth refers to the Egyptian bondage — Israel's period of national infancy as a slave people. The reproach of widowhood is the exile: the death of the husband-king, the destruction of the temple, the cessation of public religious life. Both shame categories are relational and public — they are the community's damaged reputation in the ANE world's honor economy.
54.5: For your Maker is your husband — YHWH of hosts is his name — and your Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel; he is called the God of the whole earth. The spousal metaphor for YHWH's covenant with Israel recurs through Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel. Baal worship was literally a rival-husband theology: the Canaanite fertility god as the land's husband who produced agricultural fertility. YHWH reclaims the spousal role with four titles — Maker, YHWH of hosts (divine warrior), Holy One of Israel, God of the whole earth — asserting universal jurisdiction.
54.6: The LORD has called you back like a wife who was abandoned and heartbroken — like a wife taken in youth and then rejected. In ANE law, the abandoned or divorced wife had limited legal recourse; the initiative for reconciliation had to come from the husband. YHWH's call-back is entirely his own initiative — the wife has not petitioned for return. Being taken in youth but then rejected describes Zion's covenant history: the passionate young relationship of the wilderness period (Hos 2:15) followed by the exile's rejection.
54.7: For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with great compassion I will bring you back. The temporal contrast — brief moment vs. great compassion — is a covenant proportionality argument. The Babylonian exile (587–539 BCE) was approximately 48 years; YHWH's everlasting love is measured against this. The word for compassion (rahamim) shares its root with rehem (womb): the most visceral, bodily form of parental attachment. Divine compassion is not abstract sentiment but womb-deep maternal love.
54.8: In a surge of anger I hid my face from you for a moment, but with everlasting covenant love (hesed olam) I will have compassion on you. The hidden face is the withdrawal of the divine presence — the catastrophe of the exile experienced as YHWH's absence rather than his punishment. Hesed olam (everlasting covenant loyalty) is the specific covenantal term for the binding, obligatory love of treaty relationship — not merely affection but the loyalty that the suzerain owes the vassal he has adopted. The moment of anger cannot outlast the everlasting covenant.
54.9: To me this is like the days of Noah: just as I swore that the waters of Noah would never again cover the earth, so now I swear I will never again be angry with you or rebuke you. The Noahic covenant (Gen 9:11-15) becomes the precedent and model for the new covenant. The rainbow oath was YHWH's self-binding promise to creation; the present oath is YHWH's self-binding promise to Israel. The comparison elevates the covenant-restoration to cosmic proportions: as fundamental as the post-flood guarantee of creation's continuity.
54.10: Though the mountains should move and the hills be shaken, my covenant love will not move from you, and my covenant of peace will not be shaken. Geological stability was the ANE metaphor for permanence — mountains were the most immovable features of the landscape. By asserting that YHWH's covenant love is more permanent than mountains, the text establishes a stability category beyond geology. The brit shalom (covenant of peace) is the technical term for the comprehensive shalom-relationship of suzerainty, encompassing security, prosperity, and relational wholeness.
54.11: O afflicted city, tossed by storms and without comfort — I will set your stones in brilliant color and lay your foundations with sapphires. The storm-tossed city image describes the political instability of Jerusalem post-exile: repeatedly conquered, the city lacked the settled permanence of a properly founded civic order. The sapphire foundation invokes the ANE temple-building tradition: great temples were built on cosmic foundations; the new Jerusalem's foundations will be of the most precious cosmic materials.
54.12: I will make your towers of rubies, your gates of sparkling jewels, and all your walls of precious stones. The precious-stone city vision anticipates Revelation 21:10-21 in remarkable detail. In ANE royal ideology, the elaborate ornamentation of city gates and walls demonstrated the king's wealth and the city's cosmic status. YHWH's rebuilding of Jerusalem exceeds all ANE royal construction projects by using materials that no human builder could afford or source.
54.13: All your children will be taught by the LORD (limudei YHWH), and great will be their peace. The limudim (disciples/taught ones) root connects to the Servant's discipled tongue of 50:4 and to Isaiah 8:16. The promise that all Zion's children will be YHWH's personal disciples democratizes the prophetic-school relationship. John 6:45 quotes this verse directly as the scriptural basis for being drawn to the Son: everyone taught by YHWH comes to him.
54.14: You will be established in righteousness — far from oppression and without fear; terror will not come near you. Righteousness (tzedakah) as the foundation of civic order is the standard prophetic vision of the ideal community. The absence of oppression and terror describes the restoration of the Zion ideal: the city where the socially vulnerable are safe, because YHWH's justice is the operating principle of civic life rather than the power calculations of empire.
54.15: If anyone attacks you it is not my doing — whoever assembles against you will fall before you. The verse addresses the practical concern of the returning exiles: what about enemy attacks? YHWH's disclaimer — it is not my doing — distinguishes unauthorized attacks (which he does not sanction) from the disciplinary exile (which he did). The covenant guarantee of military security for the restored community is here made explicit.
54.16: I am the one who created the blacksmith who fans the coals and forges a weapon fit for its purpose — and I am the one who created the destroyer to cause ruin. The weapon-creator claim grounds military threat within YHWH's sovereign creation. Even the empire's weapons-industry ultimately traces to YHWH's creative act: the blacksmith is his creature, the forge his provision. No weapon therefore escapes his sovereign authority, making the next verse's promise logically secure.
54.17: No weapon formed against you will succeed, and every tongue that accuses you in court you will refute. This is the inheritance of YHWH's servants, and their vindication comes from me. The covenant-lawsuit acquittal is permanent: the servants' standing in YHWH's court is established, not provisional. Inheritance (nachalah) is the land-grant term — what YHWH's servants receive as their covenantal portion is not merely protection but vindication that is definitively theirs.

55.1: Come, all who are thirsty — come to the waters; you who have no money, come, buy and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without cost (chinnam). The marketplace-crier form was familiar in ANE cities: vendors advertised goods publicly in the open bazaar. The radical inversion is that the best goods — water, wine, milk — are offered chinnam (the same gratis-word as 52:3). Water was precious in the ancient Near East; wine and milk were luxury commodities. The offer of all three without payment overturns the economics of survival and desire simultaneously.
55.2: Why do you spend money on what is not bread, and your wages on what does not satisfy? The critique targets the Babylonian economy of desire: the exile community spending on food-substitutes (idol-temple feasts, Babylonian religious participation) that provide no genuine nourishment. The metaphor of wages-for-satisfaction is an economic diagnostic — the community is getting a poor return on its spiritual investment. YHWH offers the real commodity (covenant relationship) at no cost while the false goods consume resources.
55.3: Come, hear, and you will live — and I will make an everlasting covenant with you, the sure mercies I promised to David (hasde david ha-ne'emanim). The Davidic covenant's promised mercies (hesed in the plural = repeated acts of covenant loyalty) are extended beyond the single Davidic king to the entire restored community. This democratization of the Davidic promise is one of Second Isaiah's most significant theological innovations: the covenant benefits that flowed through the royal line are redistributed to the people as a whole.
55.4: See, I made him a witness to the nations, a leader and commander for the peoples. David's historical role as international figure — the king whose fame spread to surrounding nations — is here repurposed as template for Israel's restored role. The Davidic titles (witness, leader, commander) are conferred on the covenant community in its new-Exodus configuration. Israel becomes the testimony-bearer to the nations in place of the Davidic monarchy.
55.5: You will summon a nation you did not know, and a nation that did not know you will run to you, because of YHWH your God and the Holy One of Israel, for he has glorified you. The unknown-nation coming to Israel reverses the direction of ANE imperial attraction: normally small nations were drawn to imperial capitals. The ingathering is not because of Israel's inherent attractiveness but because YHWH has glorified her — the divine reputation draws international attention to the restored community.
55.6: Seek the LORD while he may be found; call on him while he is near. The kairos warning — the appointed time of access is not permanent. This echoes the time of favor (et ratzon) of 49:8: there is a window of divine availability that must be seized. The prophetic tradition consistently insisted that the moment of YHWH's turning toward the people was an opportunity, not a permanent default condition. Missing the window is the tragedy of Israel's recurring covenant failure.
55.7: Let the wicked forsake his way and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return (shuv) to YHWH, who will have compassion on him, and to our God, who will abundantly pardon. The teshuvah (shuv = return/repentance) theology of the prophetic tradition is articulated in its fullest positive form: YHWH will abundantly (yarbe) pardon — multiplicative, not minimal forgiveness. The word pardon (lisloach) is the specific term for covenant amnesty; YHWH's pardon is not reluctant or conditional on adequate contrition but lavishly available.
55.8: For my thoughts are not your thoughts, and your ways are not my ways, declares YHWH. The incomprehensibility of divine perspective is stated as the theological ground for lavish pardon: human moral accounting would not forgive so freely. The verse connects the abundant pardon of v. 7 to the infinite qualitative gap between divine and human cognition. This is not an evasion of theodicy but an explanation for why YHWH acts in ways that seem logically improbable from within human honor-shame calculus.
55.9: For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts. The vertical spatial metaphor for the gap between divine and human cognition uses the most obvious dimension of ANE cosmology: the sky's unreachable height above the land. The heaven-earth axis was the fundamental spatial structure of the ANE worldview, with YHWH located in the highest heaven (shamayim ha-shamayim). His thoughts operate at the level of the highest heaven; human thoughts at the level of dry ground.
55.10: For as the rain and snow come down from heaven and do not return there until they have watered the earth, making it sprout and bear fruit, giving seed to the farmer and bread to the one who eats. The agricultural water cycle was one of the most carefully observed natural processes in the ancient world: rain fell, watered, produced, fed, and then evaporated back. The cycle was reliable — not random. YHWH's word has this same invariable efficacy: it does not return empty any more than precipitation returns without having watered.
55.11: So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth — it shall not return to me empty but shall accomplish what I intend and succeed in what I send it to do. The divine word as active agent is a consistent Isaianic theme: the word sent on a mission (cf. 9:8 — a word sent against Jacob). Here the word's efficacy is guaranteed by the precipitation analogy. In ANE theology, divine decrees and royal edicts were self-executing — the great king's word created the reality it described. YHWH's word operates at this level but without the failures that occasionally undercut royal decrees.
55.12: For you will go out with joy and be led forth in peace — the mountains and the hills shall burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands. The cosmic celebration of the new Exodus mirrors Isaiah 49:13 and the Psalms of creation-praise. Mountains singing and trees clapping are the natural world joining the covenant celebration. The RSV-familiar image of nature's joy is an ANE hymnic convention: creation praises YHWH's victories by personified rejoicing. The joyful, peaceful departure contrasts with the haste-and-fear of the Egyptian Exodus.
55.13: Instead of the thorn the cypress shall come up, and instead of the brier the myrtle shall come up — a memorial to YHWH, an everlasting sign that will never be cut off. The vegetation reversal (thorn → cypress, brier → myrtle) is the Eden-restoration pattern: the curse-vegetation of Genesis 3:18 gives way to the fragrant, useful trees of the renewed creation. The myrtle (hadas) was a ceremonial plant in Israel (Neh 8:15; Zech 1:8-11). The reversal becomes a permanent covenant sign embedded in the landscape.

56.1: Thus says YHWH: Maintain justice and do what is right, for my salvation is coming soon and my righteousness is about to be revealed. The ethical imperative precedes the eschatological promise: keeping mishpat and doing righteousness are the conditions of the covenant community's life, not prerequisites for earning the salvation. The salvation is already coming; the ethical life is the appropriate response to and participation in the approaching divine act.
56.2: Blessed is the man who does this and the person who holds to it — who keeps the sabbath without profaning it and refrains from doing any evil. Sabbath-keeping as the visible covenantal marker was the distinctive practice of the exilic and post-exilic community. In Babylon, where the temple calendar was impossible to maintain, the sabbath became the portable covenant sign — the one practice that identified Israel as YHWH's covenant people without requiring land, temple, or king.
56.3: Let not the foreigner who has joined himself to YHWH say, 'The LORD will surely exclude me from his people'; and let not the eunuch say, 'I am a withered tree.' The two excluded categories of Deuteronomy 23:1, 3-8 — eunuchs (v. 1) and certain foreigners (Ammonites/Moabites, vv. 3-8) — are explicitly addressed. The foreigner's fear of exclusion and the eunuch's self-identification as a withered tree (no biological legacy) represent the shame of the marginalized. The oracle overturns both exclusions.
56.4: To the eunuchs who keep my sabbaths, who choose what pleases me and hold fast to my covenant — the criteria for inclusion shift entirely from biological status to ethical and covenantal practice: sabbath-keeping, choosing YHWH's will, holding the covenant. Eunuchs in ANE royal courts (Babylon, Persia, Assyria) were often powerful administrators and confidants of the king; the Daniel narrative operates in this environment. Their exclusion from the covenant assembly despite their importance in imperial service is addressed here.
56.5: I will give them within my house and within my walls a memorial and a name (yad va-shem, literally hand and name) better than sons and daughters — an everlasting name that will never be cut off. The yad va-shem (hand and name, the phrase from which Yad Vashem draws its name) provides the eunuch with permanent covenant memorial in place of biological legacy. Sons and daughters were the normal vehicle for name-perpetuation in the ANE; the eunuch who cannot have biological children receives a name that outlasts all biological lineages.
56.6: The foreigners who join themselves to YHWH, to serve him, to love the name of YHWH, and to be his servants — everyone who keeps the sabbath without profaning it and holds fast my covenant. The three criteria for the foreigner's full inclusion — service, love of the name, sabbath-keeping — are identical to what is asked of the native Israelite. The covenant community is redefined around practice and commitment rather than genealogy, anticipating the full Gentile inclusion of the New Testament.
56.7: Even them I will bring to my holy mountain and make them glad in my house of prayer; their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar — for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples. The temple's purpose as a house of prayer for all peoples is the most universalist statement in the Hebrew Bible's temple theology. The temple was constructed with zones of exclusion (Court of Women, Court of Israel, Court of Priests) that kept non-Israelites at a distance. Jesus quotes this verse (Mark 11:17; Matt 21:13) when cleansing the temple — invoking its original intention against its corrupted present practice.
56.8: The Lord YHWH, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, declares: I will gather yet others to him besides those already gathered to him. The progressive ingathering: the restoration of the scattered Israelites is not the endpoint but the penultimate stage. Beyond the gathered Israelites, YHWH will gather yet more — the foreigner and eunuch of vv. 3-6 are the initial examples, but the others gestures toward a gathering that exceeds ethnic Israel. This is the open horizon of Second Isaiah's universalism.
56.9: All you wild animals of the field, come to devour — all you beasts of the forest. The sudden shift to an invitation to predators signals a judgment oracle against Israel's failed leaders (vv. 10-12). In ANE treaty and prophetic literature, wild animals devouring a flock signaled the complete failure of shepherds to protect their charges. The invitation to the beasts is ironic: since the watchmen have failed, the natural predators may as well come.
56.10: Israel's watchmen are all blind; none of them understand. They are all silent dogs that cannot bark — they dream, lie down, and love to sleep. The watchmen on city walls were the first line of warning against approaching enemies; the dogs patrolling at night barked at intruders. Both failures are catastrophic: blind watchmen and mute dogs leave the community undefended. The critique targets the prophetic and priestly leadership of the exile period — those who should have warned, interpreted events, and led the community's response have instead retreated into passivity.
56.11: The dogs are ravenous — never satisfied; and the shepherds have no understanding; they have all gone their own way, each after his own profit. The ravenous-dog image intensifies the critique: not merely lazy but actively self-seeking. The shepherd-for-profit trope is Ezekiel 34's theme throughout — the shepherds who feed themselves rather than the flock. The combination of pastoral failure (no understanding) and economic corruption (pursuing profit) describes a leadership class that has inverted its covenant obligations.
56.12: Come, they say, let us get wine; let us fill ourselves with strong drink — and tomorrow will be like today, even more abundant. The carpe diem materialism of the corrupt leaders — drinking today, confident tomorrow will bring more — is the ancient world's version of fatalistic hedonism. The phrase tomorrow will be like today, even more abundant captures an attitude of complacency that dismisses prophetic warning about coming judgment. Isaiah 22:13 uses similar language for Jerusalem's pre-siege revelry.

57.1: The righteous man perishes and no one takes it to heart — faithful men are taken away and no one understands. The righteous man is taken away from the evil to come. Social obliviousness to the deaths of the godly is a recurring prophetic complaint: the community fails to read the departure of the righteous as a sign. The phrase taken away from the evil to come interprets the righteous person's early death as providential rescue — they are removed before the coming judgment arrives, a mercy misread as tragedy.
57.2: He enters into peace — they rest in their resting places, each one who walked uprightly. The righteous person's death as entry into shalom is an early formulation of afterlife hope: the upright dead are in a state of rest rather than the anxious unresolved condition of those who die under judgment. The resting places are not merely graves but a condition of settled covenantal security beyond death.
57.3: But you — come here, you children of the sorceress, offspring of the adulterer and the prostitute. The diatribe shifts abruptly to the syncretistic community. Sorceress, adulterer, and prostitute are not merely moral categories but describe the three dimensions of Israel's covenant apostasy: sorcery (divination, seeking knowledge outside YHWH), adultery (the covenant faithlessness toward YHWH as husband), and prostitution (hiring out covenant loyalty to foreign powers and their gods in exchange for political security).
57.4: Whom are you mocking? Against whom do you open your mouth wide and stick out your tongue? Are you not children of rebellion, the offspring of lies? Mouth-opening and tongue-extension were ANE gestures of contempt and defiance. The mocking target is implicitly YHWH and his prophets. The designation children of rebellion (yalde pesha) and offspring of lies frames the idolatrous community's identity as constituted by their apostasy — they are what they practice.
57.5: You who inflame yourselves among the trees, under every leafy tree — who slaughter children in the ravines, under the crevices of the rocks. The high-place fertility cult worship under trees was the standard description of Canaanite religious practice that Israel adopted (Deut 12:2; Hos 4:13). Child sacrifice in the valley of Hinnom (Topheth) is the specific horror alluded to — burning children as offerings to Molech (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31). The rocky crevices as sacrifice sites suggest remote locations for hidden cult activity.
57.6: Your lot is among the smooth stones of the streambeds — they are your portion; you have poured out drink offerings to them and brought grain offerings. Should I be appeased by these things? The smooth stones of streambeds may represent the masseboth (standing stones) used in Canaanite shrine practice, or specific deity-stones associated with stream deities. Pouring libations and presenting grain offerings to rocks is the direct inversion of covenant worship: the correct offerings redirected to non-entities. YHWH's rhetorical question expects the answer: no.
57.7: On a high and lofty mountain you set your bed — you went up there to offer sacrifice. The bed on the high mountain combines the spatial location of the Baalist high places with the sexual imagery of covenant apostasy as adultery. Going up to offer sacrifice on elevated terrain was the standard Canaanite cultic practice that prophets consistently condemned as the alternative to YHWH's centralized worship.
57.8: Behind the door and the doorpost you placed your memorial — away from me you uncovered yourself, spread your bed wide, and struck a deal with them; you loved their bed and looked upon what was exposed. The doorpost memorial inverts the Passover sign (mezuzah) that YHWH commanded: instead of his name on the doorpost, an idol. The uncovering, bed-spreading, and deal-making sustain the adultery metaphor: covenant apostasy is a sexual relationship with the idols rather than YHWH.
57.9: You traveled to the king with oil and multiplied your fragrances — you sent your messengers far off, even down to Sheol. Traveling to the king with tribute oil was the standard ANE diplomatic protocol for vassal acknowledgment of a suzerain. Whether the king is a foreign power (Assyria, Babylon, Egypt) or the deity Molech, Israel has substituted a false suzerain for YHWH. The messengers sent even to Sheol — the underworld — suggests necromantic consultation: seeking guidance from the dead rather than YHWH (cf. Isa 8:19; 1 Sam 28).
57.10: You grew weary from your many journeys but you did not say, 'It is hopeless' — you found renewed strength, and so you were not weak. The tireless pursuit of idolatry despite the exhaustion it produces is the image of addiction: the spiritual energy invested in false worship renews itself because the idolater has found (false) vitality in it. This is different from the renewal of strength YHWH promises (40:31) — the idol worshiper's renewal is the self-sustaining energy of a destructive habit.
57.11: Whom did you fear and dread, that you lied and did not remember me or call it to mind? Was it not because I was silent and looked away that you have not feared me? YHWH's silence during the exile period was interpreted by the apostates as his permanent absence or impotence. The divine non-intervention that seemed to validate the idol-worship was actually YHWH's patience. His silence was mistaken for consent; his looking away was mistaken for defeat.
57.12: I will expose your righteousness and your deeds — but they will not benefit you. The ironic your righteousness (tzidkatekh) refers to the formal religious practice the community considers meritorious — their temple-cult performances and idol-worship — which YHWH will expose as the sham it is. The moral inventory of ANE legal proceedings exposed the defendant's deeds before the judge; here YHWH conducts that examination and finds the self-styled righteousness worthless.
57.13: When you cry out, let your collection of idols rescue you — the wind will carry them all off, a breath will sweep them away. But whoever takes refuge in me will possess the land and inherit my holy mountain. The wind-dispersal of idols is the anti-idol polemic's most devastating image: the gods you trusted cannot even maintain their own stability against a breeze. The pivot in the second half of the verse establishes the alternative: those who take refuge in YHWH receive the inheritance that the idol-worshipers will lose — land and holy mountain.
57.14: A voice will say: Build up, build up, prepare the way — clear every obstruction from the road for my people. The highway-preparation command recapitulates 40:3-4 and 62:10. The road-clearing for the returning exiles was the standard royal infrastructure task in the ANE: great kings cleared, leveled, and maintained roads for processions and military movements. YHWH's highway-preparation surpasses all royal road-building because it removes every obstacle for the covenant community's return.
57.15: For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity, whose name is Holy: I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with the one who has a contrite and humble spirit, to revive the spirit of the humble and to revive the heart of the contrite. The divine paradox of simultaneous transcendence and immanence: YHWH inhabits eternity in the highest cosmic space AND dwells with the crushed (daka) and lowly of spirit. The word contrite shares a root with the Servant's crushing in 53:5 — YHWH specifically inhabits the space of the shattered.
57.16: For I will not contend forever, nor will I be angry forever — for the spirit would faint before me and the breath of life that I have created. Divine anger is self-limiting not because of its weakness but because of the fragility of its object. If YHWH's anger were permanent and total, the created spirits he has made would disintegrate: they cannot survive unrelenting divine wrath. The restraint of judgment is therefore an act of preservation consistent with YHWH's creator role.
57.17: Because of the iniquity of his greed I was angry; I struck him; I hid my face and was angry — but he kept going, backsliding along the way of his own heart. The hidden-face withdrawal of divine presence did not produce repentance but escalating apostasy. The pattern — discipline → continued sin — is the diagnosis of the exile community's spiritual condition. YHWH struck, hid his face, and the people simply continued on their own path: the discipline did not achieve its corrective purpose.
57.18: I have seen his ways, yet I will heal him — I will lead him and restore comfort to him and to those who mourn with him. Divine healing despite observed waywardness is the covenant mercy that transcends the principle of proportional response. YHWH sees the backsliding (v. 17) and responds not with further punishment but with healing. The leading and comforting are shepherding actions: YHWH as the good shepherd who retrieves the wandering flock rather than abandoning them to their own path.
57.19: Creating the fruit of the lips — peace, peace, to the far and to the near, says YHWH, and I will heal them. YHWH creates speech — the fruit of the lips — as an act parallel to his creation of the physical world. The double shalom shalom (peace, peace) is the emphatic doubled blessing: comprehensive covenant wellbeing extended to both those near (the returned exiles) and those far (the diaspora still scattered). Ephesians 2:17 quotes this verse as fulfilled in Christ: he preached peace to those far away and peace to those who were near.
57.20: But the wicked are like the churning sea — it cannot be still, and its waters stir up mire and mud. The chaos-sea imagery recurs from 51:9-10 but now applied to the wicked within the community: they have the inner agitation of the restless deep, unable to achieve the shalom promised to the humble and contrite. The churning that produces mire and mud is the moral pollution generated by the absence of covenant peace.
57.21: There is no peace, says my God, for the wicked. The refrain appears at 48:22 and here at 57:21, forming a deliberate structural bracket around the central section of chapters 49–57. The no peace for the wicked declaration is not a threat of future punishment but a present diagnosis: the covenantal condition of shalom is structurally unavailable to those who reject the covenant. Peace is not a reward withheld; it is the natural consequence of covenant relationship that the wicked have excluded themselves from.

58.1: Cry out loudly — do not hold back; raise your voice like a trumpet and tell my people their rebellion and the house of Jacob their sins. The shofar (trumpet) was used for covenant assembly, alarm, and proclamation. Using the prophetic voice like a trumpet signals that what follows is a formal covenant-lawsuit indictment (rib). The paradox established immediately: the community being indicted considers itself righteously observant. Calling it my people while indicting them maintains the covenant relationship while naming its violation.
58.2: Yet day after day they seek me and are eager to know my ways — as though they were a nation that practices righteousness and has not abandoned the law of their God; they ask of me just decrees and are pleased to draw near to God. The devastating description of sincere-seeming religious performance without ethical content. The seeking, eagerness, and pleasure in drawing near are genuine emotional states — this is not cynical hypocrisy but sincere religiosity disconnected from justice. They ask for just decrees while practicing injustice; the disconnection is not noticed from inside the practice.
58.3: They say, 'Why have we fasted but you do not notice? Why have we denied ourselves and you take no account of it?' Look — on your fast day you pursue your own interests and push all your laborers. The Day of Atonement fast (and other prescribed fast days) required economic suspension: no work, no commerce. But the people are using the fast day to press their laborers for productivity — the denial of self is performed religiously while economic exploitation continues. YHWH notices the fast and the exploitation simultaneously.
58.4: Look, you fast only to quarrel and brawl and to strike with a wicked fist. You cannot fast as you do today and expect your voice to be heard on high. The social violence accompanying the fasting nullifies the fasting's religious efficacy: the acts of worship are performed alongside the acts of oppression. ANE prayer and sacrifice were understood to be heard by the deity when performed in the correct ritual manner; YHWH declares that no ritual correctness can make audible a prayer accompanied by violence against the vulnerable.
58.5: Is this the kind of fast I have chosen — merely a day for people to deny themselves, to bow their heads like a reed and spread sackcloth and ashes beneath them? Is that what you call a fast, a day acceptable to the LORD? The reed-bowing posture and sackcloth-and-ashes are the standard ANE and Israelite penitential gestures. YHWH's question is not rhetorical rejection of ritual form but exposure of form without content: the posture of repentance without the reorientation of power toward justice is theater, not worship.
58.6: Is not this the fast I choose: to release the bonds of injustice, to untie the ropes of the yoke, to set the oppressed free, and to break every yoke? The Jubilee fast — the Day of Atonement of Leviticus 25 announced the Jubilee year of release — is the template here: debt-slavery release, oppression freedom, yoke-breaking. YHWH's chosen fast is an economic and social event, not merely a personal religious exercise. The verbal cluster (release, untie, set free, break) is liberation vocabulary.
58.7: Is it not to share your food with the hungry, to bring the wandering poor into your home, to clothe the naked when you see them, and not to look away from your own kin? The three acts of practical justice — food-sharing, housing the displaced, clothing the naked — constitute the authentic fast. Not looking away from your own kin is the covenant obligation of family solidarity extended to the community: the poor neighbor is flesh (basar) — literally bodily kin. Averting the eyes from need is itself a covenant violation.
58.8: Then your light will break out like the dawn and your recovery will come quickly — your righteousness will lead the way before you, and the glory of the LORD will be your rear guard. The light-at-dawn metaphor captures the sudden, unmistakable appearance of covenant blessing after the darkness of injustice. The rear guard is YHWH's glory — the same pillar of cloud and fire that protected Israel at the Exodus (Exod 14:19-20). The new-Exodus pattern: covenant justice produces the divine presence that led and protected the first Exodus community.
58.9: Then you will call and YHWH will answer — you will cry and he will say, 'Here I am' (hineni). If you remove the yoke from among you, stop the pointing finger, and stop speaking wickedness. The hineni (here I am) is the divine response to the human call — the covenant call-and-answer restored.
