The pointing finger (shalach etzba) describes the condescending gesture of accusation or contempt toward the vulnerable: the social habit of marking others as inferior or culpable. Removing it is part of dismantling the oppressive social structure the fast was masking.
58.10: If you give yourself to the hungry and satisfy the needs of the oppressed — then your light will rise in the darkness and your deepest night will become as bright as noon. The self-giving (tafiq le-ra'ev nafshekha — literally pour out your soul to the hungry) is the extreme form of food-sharing: not giving from surplus but from the self. The darkness-to-noon reversal describes the exile's darkness being illuminated by justice — the ethical act produces the eschatological transformation.
58.11: The LORD will guide you continually and satisfy you in dry places and strengthen your bones — you will be like a well-watered garden, like a spring whose waters never run dry. The wilderness-provision imagery (guidance in dry places, satisfaction when parched) recapitulates the Exodus experience as template for the restored community's life. The well-watered garden is Eden-language; the spring that never runs dry is the covenant relationship's inexhaustible provision. Dry places that become gardens is the reversal at the heart of all Second Isaiah's restoration promises.
58.12: Your people will rebuild the ancient ruins — you will raise up the foundations of many generations; you will be called the Repairer of the Breach (Goder Peres), the Restorer of Paths to Live In (Meshovev Netivot). The specific titles — Repairer of the Breach and Restorer of Paths — are covenant-restoration functions. The breach (peres) in a city wall represented the point of maximum vulnerability and the evidence of defeat. Repairing it restored defensibility and honor simultaneously. Restoring paths is infrastructure repair that enables community life. The community that practices justice inherits these constructive roles.
58.13: If you keep your feet from breaking the Sabbath and refrain from doing as you please on my holy day — if you call the Sabbath a delight and the LORD's holy day worthy of honor; if you honor it by not following your own ways or pursuing your own pleasures or talking idly. The Sabbath oracle closes the chapter by returning to the covenantal marker of identity. The multiple negatives — not breaking, not doing own pleasure, not talking idly — define Sabbath as the structured suspension of self-will. The positive reframing as delight (oneg) is the distinctive Isaianic contribution: Sabbath is not merely obligation but joy.
58.14: Then you will find your joy in the LORD, and I will make you ride on the heights of the earth — I will nourish you with the heritage of Jacob your father, for the mouth of the LORD has spoken. Riding on the heights of the earth is the dominion imagery of Deuteronomy 32:13 — YHWH making Israel ride the high places of the land in the conquest. The heritage of Jacob is the patriarchal land-promise in its fullest form. The oracle closes with the prophetic authentication formula (ki pi YHWH diber) — the mouth of YHWH has spoken — marking the promise as irrevocable.

59.1: The LORD's arm is not too short to save, nor his ear too dull to hear. The verse establishes that the problem is not on YHWH's side: his power (arm) has not diminished and his attention (ear) has not faded. The short-arm metaphor addresses the exile community's temptation to conclude that YHWH was defeated by Babylon's gods or had simply become indifferent. Both inferences are explicitly denied before the indictment begins.
59.2: Your iniquities have created a barrier between you and your God, and your sins have caused him to hide his face so that he does not hear. The partition created by sin is both spatial (a wall between the parties) and relational (averted face). The hidden face is YHWH's withdrawal of the covenant presence — the reversal of the Aaronic blessing's YHWH make his face shine upon you (Num 6:25). The exile's silence from heaven is explained as iniquity-induced separation, not divine abandonment.
59.3: Your hands are stained with blood and your fingers with guilt — your lips have spoken lies and your tongue mutters deceit. The covenant-lawsuit indictment catalogs the body's complicity in covenant violation: hands, fingers, lips, tongue. Blood-guilt was the most serious covenant impurity in Israelite law (Num 35:33: blood pollutes the land). The pairing of physical violence (blood) with speech-crime (lies, deceit) covers both the social and legal dimensions of covenant failure.
59.4: No one calls for justice; no one pleads with integrity — they depend on worthless arguments and speak lies; they conceive evil and give birth to wickedness. The covenant community's judicial system is the indictment's target: the rib (legal plea) process has been corrupted. Conceiving evil and giving birth to wickedness is the agricultural metaphor applied to the moral-legal process — the community produces wickedness as a natural crop. Paul uses this passage (vv. 7-8) in Romans 3:15-17 as part of his universal-condemnation argument.
59.5: They hatch the eggs of vipers and weave a spider's web — whoever eats their eggs will die, and if one is cracked, a poisonous snake emerges. The double natural-world image of hatching vipers and weaving webs describes the community's activity as inherently lethal and futile: viper eggs hatch into more vipers, and spider webs trap but provide no genuine shelter. The hatching imagery implies that the evil was incubated and produced over time — systemic corruption, not isolated incidents.
59.6: Their webs will not serve as clothing — they cannot cover themselves with what they weave. Their works are works of wickedness, and their hands carry out violence. The spider-web as failed clothing captures the self-defeating nature of the wicked community's projects: what they create cannot protect or cover them. In the ANE, clothing was both practical and social — the garment defined status and identity. A covering that cannot cover is both physically useless and socially humiliating.
59.7: Their feet rush toward evil and they are quick to shed innocent blood — their thoughts are plots of wickedness; ruin and destruction mark their ways. The totality of the body's orientation toward evil — feet running, thoughts plotting — shows the comprehensive moral capture of the community. Paul quotes these verses (vv. 7-8) in Romans 3:15-17 as universal human condemnation: Their feet are swift to shed blood; ruin and misery are in their paths.
59.8: They do not know the way of peace — there is no justice in their tracks; they have bent every path crooked, and no one who walks them knows peace. The absence of the way of peace (derekh shalom) is both descriptive and ironic: the covenant community, supposed mediators of YHWH's shalom to the nations, have made every path crooked. No one who walks their bent paths finds the shalom that their covenant vocation was meant to produce and share.
59.9: Therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not reach us — we wait for light, but there is only darkness; for brightness, but we walk in deep shadow. The pronoun shifts from accusation (they, vv. 3-8) to confession (we, vv. 9-15): the community owns the indictment. Waiting for light while experiencing darkness is the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:29 realized: At midday you shall grope as the blind grope in darkness. The community is inside the curse they brought on themselves.
59.10: We grope along the wall like blind men — we fumble like those with no eyes; we stumble at midday as in the dusk; among the healthy we are like the dead. The blindness-at-noon is a direct echo of the Deuteronomy 28:28-29 covenant curse: YHWH will strike with blindness, causing groping at noon. The community experiencing this curse is simultaneously fulfilling the Mosaic warning and confessing its cause. Among the healthy they are like the dead — the living-dead status of those under covenant judgment.
59.11: We all growl like bears and moan like doves — we look for justice but there is none; for deliverance but it is far away. Bear-growling and dove-moaning are complementary lament sounds: the bear's aggressive rumble and the dove's plaintive coo. The dove was associated with mourning throughout the ANE (cf. Isa 38:14). Looking for mishpat and finding none is the most fundamental covenant failure: the community in which YHWH's justice should be visible has emptied itself of it.
59.12: For our rebellions are multiplied before you and our sins testify against us — we are deeply aware of our transgressions and we know our own iniquities. The covenant lawsuit reaches its formal confession stage: the defendants admit the evidence. Testify against us uses the covenant-witness language of the suzerainty treaty: the witnesses of the covenant now testify to its violation. Self-awareness of sin is the beginning of the lament's movement toward the divine rescue announced in v. 16.
59.13: We have rebelled against and denied the LORD — we have turned away from following our God; we have spoken oppression and treachery, conceived and repeated from our hearts words of falsehood. The four verbs of apostasy (rebelled, denied, turned away, spoken falsehood) cover the full compass of covenant violation: internal disposition (turning away), speech-act (denying), and habitual pattern (conceiving and repeating). The heart as the source of falsehood parallels Jeremiah 17:9's diagnosis of the human heart as incurably deceitful.
59.14: Justice has been driven back, and righteousness stands at a distance — truth has fallen in the streets, and honesty cannot get through. The personification of the civic virtues as exiles — justice driven back, righteousness at a distance, truth fallen, honesty unable to enter — is the rhetorical brilliance of the passage. The virtues that should inhabit the covenant community's public life have themselves been expelled: they are now external observers of the community's collapse rather than its operating principles.
59.15: Truth has vanished, and anyone who turns from evil exposes himself to attack. YHWH saw it and it displeased him that there was no justice. The social inversion is complete: the righteous person is now the dangerous outsider rather than the community norm. YHWH's displeasure (yera' be-einav — it was evil in his eyes) at the absence of mishpat sets up the divine initiative of v. 16: when no human champion of justice exists, YHWH himself must act.
59.16: He saw that there was no man, and was astounded that there was no one to intervene — so his own arm achieved salvation for him, and his own righteousness upheld him. The astonishment at human absence echoes 63:5 and parallels the motif in Isaiah 41:28 and 50:2 — YHWH looking for a human partner and finding none. The divine arm achieving salvation alone is the pattern of the Exodus (YHWH fighting for Israel with no human army), now repeated: the new Exodus depends entirely on YHWH's unilateral action. Paul develops this in Romans 11:26 where the Redeemer comes from Zion.
59.17: He put on righteousness as a breastplate and a helmet of salvation on his head — clothed himself in garments of vengeance and wrapped himself in zeal like a cloak. The divine warrior's armor is entirely his own attributes: righteousness, salvation, vengeance, zeal. This is not conventional ANE military equipment but YHWH's own character serving as his battle gear. Paul adapts this in Ephesians 6:14-17 (the armor of God) and 1 Thessalonians 5:8, where the believer is exhorted to put on the same divine attributes.
59.18: According to what they have done, so he will repay — fury to his foes, punishment to his enemies; he will pay back even the distant coastlands. The covenant principle of proportional repayment (gemul ke-gemul) governs the divine warrior's action: enemies receive exactly what their deeds warrant. The extension of repayment to the distant coastlands (the ends of the known world) globalizes the divine justice — no enemy of YHWH's covenant purpose escapes his reach.
59.19: From the west they will fear the name of the LORD and from the east his glory — for he will come like a rushing torrent driven by the Spirit of the LORD. Universal fear of YHWH's name from all compass points follows the divine warrior's comprehensive victory. The rushing torrent (literally: the enemy will come like a river) driven by the Spirit suggests the divine Breath (ruach YHWH) as the propulsive force behind YHWH's redemptive action — the Spirit driving the new Exodus as it had driven the original creation (Gen 1:2).
59.20: A Redeemer (go'el) will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from their rebellion. The Redeemer-to-Zion announcement is the climax of the oracle. The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) comes to buy back the alienated family member and property. The qualification — to those who turn from rebellion — introduces the teshuvah condition. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 11:26 as the scriptural warrant for Israel's ultimate salvation: The Deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob.
59.21: This is my covenant with them: my Spirit that is upon you and my words that I have placed in your mouth shall not leave your mouth, nor the mouth of your children, nor the mouth of your grandchildren — from now on and forever. The new covenant is defined as the permanent indwelling of Spirit and word across three generations and then forever. This is the Jeremiah 31:31-34 and Ezekiel 36:26-27 vision in Second Isaiah's formulation: the internal, permanent, and transmitted presence of YHWH's Spirit and word as the distinguishing mark of the restored covenant community.

60.1: Arise, shine, for your light has come — the glory of the LORD has risen over you. The double imperative (arise, shine) summons Zion to active response to an event that has already occurred: the light has come, the glory has risen. The light is not natural sunlight but the divine kavod (glory-cloud) — the same presence that filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35) and Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11). Zion's rising and shining is her reflection of a light she did not generate.
60.2: For darkness covers the earth and thick darkness the peoples — but the LORD will rise over you and his glory will appear upon you. The contrast between the world's covering darkness and Zion's specific illumination creates a cosmic chiaroscuro: the nations are in deep darkness while YHWH's glory-cloud rises over Zion. The verb will appear upon you (yera'eh alayikh) is the theophany-appearance verb — YHWH making himself visible as he appeared to Moses and the patriarchs.
60.3: Nations will come to your light and kings to the brightness of your dawn. The centripetal movement of the nations toward Zion reverses the exile's centrifugal dispersal of Israel. ANE royal ideology described great cities as attracting the tribute and attention of surrounding peoples; here Zion's drawing power is not military conquest but the divine light it reflects. Kings making pilgrimage to Jerusalem recurs as an eschatological image throughout the prophets (Mic 4:2; Zech 14:16).
60.4: Lift your eyes all around and look — they are all assembling and coming to you; your sons will come from far away, and your daughters will be carried on the arm. The lift-and-look command summons Zion to observe the gathering ingathering. Sons coming from afar and daughters carried on the arm mirrors the repatriation language of 49:22 — the nations become the transport service for Israel's scattered children. The nursing-on-the-arm image captures the care and tenderness of the return.
60.5: Then you will see and be radiant — your heart will thrill and overflow, because the wealth of the seas will be channeled to you and the riches of the nations will come to you. The wealth-of-seas and riches-of-nations reversal inverts the tribute system that had Israel paying Assyria and Babylon. The Mediterranean sea-trade networks (Phoenician, Greek, later Carthaginian) are envisioned as directing their commerce toward Jerusalem. Zion's heart overflowing (yarḥav) is the expansiveness of joy that transcends normal emotional containment.
60.6: A great herd of camels will cover your land — the young camels of Midian and Ephah; all from Sheba will come, bringing gold and incense and proclaiming the praises of the LORD. The Midian-Ephah-Sheba caravan trade route was the spice-and-gold highway of the ancient world, running from the Arabian peninsula through Transjordan. Sheba's queen brought gold and spices to Solomon (1 Kgs 10:2); now the entire Sabaean trade network is reoriented toward the restored Jerusalem. Matthew 2:11 connects the Magi's gold and incense explicitly to this verse.
60.7: All the flocks of Kedar shall be gathered to you — the rams of Nebaioth shall serve you; they shall come up as acceptable offerings on my altar, and I will adorn my glorious house. Kedar and Nebaioth were the dominant pastoral tribes of the northwestern Arabian peninsula (sons of Ishmael, Gen 25:13). Their flocks, known for their quality, become the sacrificial livestock for the restored temple. The temple YHWH will adorn (aphar) anticipates the new temple's surpassing beauty, fulfilling Haggai 2:9.
60.8: Who are these that fly along like a cloud, like doves returning to their nests? The astonishment-question is the literary form of sudden, unexpected abundance: the questioner cannot identify the source of the vast, rapid return. Flying like a cloud describes the visual density of a large group moving quickly — the dust cloud of returning caravan-loads. Doves returning to their nests is the instinctive homing movement: the diaspora drawn back to Zion as inevitably as birds to their roost.
60.9: Surely the coastlands wait for me, and the ships of Tarshish are first — to bring your children from afar, their silver and gold with them, for the name of the LORD your God and for the Holy One of Israel. Tarshish (possibly ancient Tartessos in Spain or Sardinia) represented the western extremity of the Mediterranean trade world. Tarshish-ships were the largest merchant vessels of the Iron Age. That the westernmost sea-power leads the fleet of returnees frames the ingathering as encompassing the entire width of the known world.
60.10: Foreigners will rebuild your walls and their kings will serve you — though in my anger I struck you, in my compassion I have shown mercy to you. The reversal is explicit: the foreign nations whose armies demolished Jerusalem's walls (2 Kgs 25:10) are now the construction workforce rebuilding them. The theological logic is YHWH's own proportionality statement — anger-moment was brief; compassion-mercy is the dominant condition. The Cyrus decree (Ezra 1-6) is a partial fulfillment; the full vision exceeds any single historical event.
60.11: Your gates will stand open continually — they will not be shut by day or night, so that people may bring you the wealth of the nations, with their kings escorted in. Perpetually open city gates were architecturally impossible in the ANE — gates were the primary defensive structure, shut at night and in danger. YHWH's vision of permanently open gates signals the complete elimination of the threat that made gate-closure necessary. Revelation 21:25 quotes this directly: the new Jerusalem's gates are never shut.
60.12: For the nation and kingdom that will not serve you shall be destroyed — such nations shall be completely devastated. The warning to resistant nations is the covenant obverse of the promise to the serving nations: service to Zion brings blessing; refusal brings destruction. This is not Israelite triumphalism but the ANE suzerainty logic applied eschatologically — the great king's vassal nations must serve or face annihilation from the great king's forces.
60.13: The splendor of Lebanon will come to you — the juniper, the fir, and the box tree together — to adorn the place of my sanctuary, and I will make the place where my feet rest glorious. The Lebanese forest timbers (cedar, juniper, fir, box) were the premium building material of the entire ANE: Phoenician cedar was used in the temples of Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Israel. Solomon's first temple used Lebanon cedar extensively (1 Kgs 5:6-8). The renewed sanctuary will surpass Solomon's by receiving the same materials as a voluntary tribute from the nations.
60.14: The descendants of those who tormented you will come bowing down before you — all who despised you will prostrate themselves at your feet; they will call you the City of the LORD, the Zion of the Holy One of Israel. The prostration of former oppressors is the ANE military-submission gesture applied eschatologically: the conquered peoples depicted on Assyrian and Babylonian reliefs (feet of kings used as footstools) now direct that posture toward Zion. The renaming — City of the LORD — is the covenantal honor-restoration that the nations themselves confer.
60.15: Although you have been forsaken and despised, with no one traveling through you — I will make you an everlasting pride and a source of joy for all generations. The azuvah (forsaken) of v. 15 directly anticipates the name-change in 62:4: Zion called Forsaken will be renamed Hephzibah (My Delight). The isolation of no travelers through her gates — the commercial and social deadness of the destroyed city — is reversed into an everlasting joy that draws all generations.
60.16: You will drink the milk of the nations and nurse at the breast of kings — and you will know that I, YHWH, am your Savior, your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob. The nursing metaphor inverts the normal power relationship: Israel as the infant nourished by the great nations and their kings, rather than Israel as tribute-payer to those same kings. The Mighty One of Jacob (Avir Ya'akov) is an ancient divine title (Gen 49:24) connecting the restoration to the patriarchal promise.
60.17: Instead of bronze I will bring gold — instead of iron, silver; instead of wood, bronze, and instead of stones, iron. I will make peace your governor and righteousness your ruler. The ascending metals sequence (stones → iron → bronze → silver → gold) represents the progressive upgrading of all civic materials: the restored community will be built of better stuff than any previous order. Peace (shalom) as governor and righteousness as ruler personify the governing principles of the eschatological community — not kings or prefects but YHWH's own attributes administering the city.
60.18: Violence will no longer be heard in your land, nor ruin or destruction within your borders — you will name your walls Salvation and your gates Praise. The city's architectural features renamed as theological declarations: walls of Salvation (yeshu'ah) and gates of Praise (tehillah). In ANE cities, gates were named for events, rulers, or deities. Naming the gates Praise and the walls Salvation transforms the city's very infrastructure into continuous liturgical proclamation.
60.19: The sun will no longer be your light by day, nor will the brightness of the moon give you light — but the LORD will be your everlasting light and your God will be your glory. The replacement of the natural luminaries by divine presence is the most radical element of the eschatological vision: the sun and moon — the two great lights of Genesis 1:16 — become functionally unnecessary when YHWH is the direct light-source. Revelation 21:23-24 quotes this precisely: the new Jerusalem has no need of sun or moon because the glory of God illuminates it.
60.20: Your sun will never set again, nor will your moon wane — for the LORD will be your everlasting light, and your days of grief will be over. The permanent-light condition eliminates the grief-seasons associated with cyclical darkness: mourning at sunset, fear at new moon, lamentation during natural dimming. The ANE mourning calendar was organized around natural cycles; YHWH's permanent presence abolishes grief-time itself. The days of mourning are over because the occasion for mourning — distance from YHWH — no longer exists.
60.21: Your people will all be righteous — they will possess the land forever; the shoot I have planted, the work of my hands, so that I might be glorified. The democratization of righteousness: the entire restored community, not just the leaders or the priestly class, will be righteous. The shoot (netzer) planted by YHWH connects to the Davidic branch of 11:1 (netzer from Jesse's roots) and anticipates the Servant's planting metaphor of 61:3. YHWH's glory as the purpose of the community's righteousness grounds the promise in divine rather than human initiative.
60.22: The least will become a thousand and the smallest a mighty nation — I, YHWH, will bring it to pass at the right time. The one-to-thousand multiplication is the Abrahamic covenant's demographic promise in its most compressed form: from one ancestor, a nation; from the smallest, the mightiest. The kairos formula — at the right time (be'ittah aḥishenah) — affirms YHWH's sovereign timing over the promise's fulfillment. The right moment is his to determine, not predictable by human calculation.

62.1: For Zion's sake I will not keep silent and for Jerusalem's sake I will not rest — until her righteousness shines out as brightness and her salvation blazes like a torch. The speaker (YHWH or the prophet) commits to persistent, unsilenced advocacy for Jerusalem. The double metaphors of shining brightness and blazing torch are light-intensity images: the righteousness must be unmistakably, brilliantly visible to the watching nations. The refusal to be silent or rest is the posture of the importunate petitioner who does not cease until the answer comes.
62.2: The nations will see your righteousness and all the kings your glory — you will be called by a new name that the LORD himself will bestow. The new name is a divine act, not a human choice: YHWH himself renames Zion, just as he renamed Abram (Abraham) and Jacob (Israel). The name-gift is an act of identity-creation — the new name defines the new covenant status rather than merely describing it. The nations and kings as witnesses to the new name-giving makes the covenant restoration a public international event.
62.3: You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of the LORD and a royal diadem in the hand of your God. The crown (ateret tiferet) and royal diadem (tzniph melukhah) are both in YHWH's hand — Zion is displayed as his most prized possession rather than worn on his head. The image is of a jeweler holding up a gem to show its beauty to others. ANE kings wore their crown as the most visible symbol of their majesty; YHWH holds his crown-jewel Zion up for all to see.
62.4: You will no longer be called Forsaken (Azuvah) nor your land Desolate (Shemamah) — but you will be called My Delight Is in Her (Hephzibah) and your land Married (Beulah); for the LORD delights in you and your land will be wedded. The four names are formal covenant-status designations: Azuvah and Shemamah describe exile's condition; Hephzibah (the actual name of Hezekiah's wife, 2 Kgs 21:1) and Beulah describe the restored condition. ANE cities and nations were often named for their political or covenant status; the name-change is simultaneously linguistic and ontological.
62.5: For as a young man marries a young woman, so your sons will marry you — and as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you. The double marriage metaphor: the sons of Zion are the bridegroom who joyfully inhabits the restored city; YHWH himself is the bridegroom who rejoices over his bride. The word for rejoices (yassis) is the exuberant joy of the wedding celebration — the most festive social event in the ANE calendar. YHWH's rejoicing over Zion is the eschatological climax of the covenant relationship.
62.6: On your walls, O Jerusalem, I have stationed watchmen — they will never be silent day or night. You who remind the LORD, give yourselves no rest. The watchmen are intercessors, not military sentinels: their job is to never let YHWH forget his promise to restore Jerusalem. The mazkirim (those who remind) is a specific Hebrew term for the royal remembrancer — an official whose duty was to keep the king's obligations before him. These intercessory watchmen are divine appointees keeping YHWH's own promise before him.
62.7: And give him no rest until he establishes Jerusalem and makes her a glory in the earth. The shockingly bold language of the persistent intercessor: giving YHWH no rest. This inverts the normal logic of petition — the petitioner is usually exhausted before the king responds. Here the intercessors are commanded to persist until the answer comes, and YHWH himself appoints them to this unceasing advocacy. The command mirrors Jesus's parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1-8) — importunate prayer as the appropriate covenantal posture.
62.8: The LORD has sworn by his right hand and by his strong arm: Never again will I give your grain to your enemies as food, nor will foreigners drink the new wine for which you have labored. The divine oath-gesture — right hand and strong arm — is the most binding self-invocation available in the ANE. YHWH swears by his own power (having nothing greater to swear by, cf. Heb 6:13). The content of the oath addresses the specific economic humiliation of tribute: the harvest Israel raised paid foreign tribute-collectors. The oath reverses this permanently.
62.9: But those who have gathered it will eat it and praise the LORD — and those who bring it in will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary. The covenant meal within the sanctuary precincts is the restoration of the proper social order: the producer eats and praises YHWH; the harvest is consumed in the place of worship rather than in the tribute-halls of foreign emperors. The sanctuary meal is both economic justice and liturgical act — the two are inseparable in the restored covenant community.
62.10: Pass through, pass through the gates — prepare the way for the people; build up the highway, clear it of stones; raise a banner over the nations. The highway-preparation command recapitulates 40:3-4 and 57:14. The banner (nes) raised over the nations is the military standard that signals the gathering of the nations to YHWH's cause. Isaiah 11:10-12 also uses the banner-for-nations image as the Davidic signal for the second gathering of Israel's scattered remnant.
62.11: The LORD has declared to the ends of the earth: Say to daughter Zion, 'See, your salvation is coming — his reward is with him and his recompense before him.' The universal declaration to the ends of the earth echoes 40:9-10 and 52:7. The salvation arriving with its reward and recompense describes the divine warrior returning from victory: he carries the spoils (sekar) and the distribution (pe'ulah) of his campaign. Revelation 22:12 echoes this: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me.
62.12: They will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of the LORD — and you will be named Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken. The four titles of the restored community: Holy People (am ha-kodesh), Redeemed of the LORD (ge'ulei YHWH), Sought Out (derushshah), City Not Forsaken (lo ne'ezavah). These names reverse the exile's shaming: the abandoned city becomes the city actively sought; the profane becomes the holy; the unredeemed become the go'el-redeemed. The naming closes the chapter as the culmination of the honor-restoration process.

63.1: Who is this coming from Edom, with garments reddened from Bozrah — this one glorious in his attire, striding forward in his great strength? Bozrah was the Edomite capital (modern Buseira in Jordan). Edom in prophetic literature (Obad; Jer 49; Ezek 35) was the representative enemy who exploited Jerusalem's fall in 586 BCE, blocking fugitives and celebrating the destruction (Obad 11-14; Ps 137:7). The divine warrior arriving from Edom with blood-stained garments is returning from executing judgment on Israel's most treacherous historical neighbor.
63.2: Why are your garments red, as if you had been treading in a winepress? The question from the observer establishes the interpretive frame: the red garments need explanation. Winepress treading was a communal harvest activity where workers stripped off their robes and tread the grapes barefoot — the grape juice stained everything red. The winepress image for judgment appears in Joel 3:13 and Revelation 14:19-20, which quotes this passage directly.
63.3: I have trodden the winepress alone, and from the nations no one helped me — I trod them in my anger and trampled them in my fury; their blood is sprinkled on my garments, and I have stained all my clothing. The divine warrior's solitary action echoes 59:16 and 63:5: YHWH looked for a human helper and found none, so his own arm acted alone. The identification of the grape-juice stains with blood reframes the winepress as a battle: the nations are the grapes, their blood the divine warrior's harvest. The sprinkle (yazzeh) echo connects to the Servant's sprinkling of nations in 52:15.
63.4: For the day of vengeance was in my heart and the year of my redemption had arrived. The pairing of vengeance-day and redemption-year is the same dual action as 61:2 (year of favor + day of vengeance). The divine warrior's inner conviction — carried in his heart through the period of Israel's suffering — now becomes outward action. The year of redemption (shenat ge'ulai) is the Jubilee vocabulary again: the appointed time when the kinsman-redeemer acts.
63.5: I looked, but there was no one to help — I was appalled that there was none to sustain me; so my own arm brought salvation, and my fury sustained me. The third iteration of the no-human-helper motif (cf. 59:16; here again). YHWH's appallment (va'eshtomaim) at the absence of helpers is a divine emotional response to human absence — not weakness but the appropriate reaction to finding a partner-shaped void. His own arm and his own fury become the substitutes for the human partnership that would have been the fitting response.
63.6: I trampled the peoples in my anger — I made them drunk in my fury, and I poured their blood down to the ground. The winepress metaphor completes: the nations made drunk with the divine fury-wine are the grapes crushed in the press. Pouring blood to the ground is both the ritual of blood-disposal (Lev 17:13 — blood poured on the ground as the soul returned to earth) and the image of total defeat. The divine warrior's comprehensive victory leaves no enemy standing.
63.7: I will recount the steadfast lovingkindnesses (hasde YHWH) of the LORD and his praises — for all that the LORD has bestowed on us, the great goodness to the house of Israel according to his compassion and his abundant steadfast love. The transition from the divine warrior's victory speech to historical confession and lament is abrupt but structurally coherent: the God who just acted alone in judgment is also the God of Israel's covenantal history. The plural hasde (loyal covenant acts in the plural) catalogs the accumulated divine faithfulness across generations.
63.8: For he said, 'Surely they are my people, children who will not prove false' — and so he became their Savior. YHWH's initial confidence in Israel at the Exodus election is recalled here: the covenant-establishing declaration that these are his people. The expectation that they will not prove false (lo yeshaqeru) is the covenantal assumption that was historically violated. YHWH's savior-role follows from his election-declaration: having claimed them as his, he is bound to save them.
63.9: In all their affliction he was afflicted, and the angel of his presence saved them — in his love and compassion he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them through all the days of old. The angel of his presence (mal'ach panav) is the personified divine presence in the Exodus pillar — the representation of YHWH's face going with Israel. The identification of YHWH with Israel's suffering (be-khol tzaratam lo tzar) is one of the most striking expressions of divine empathy in the entire OT: his people's adversity was his own adversity. He carried them through the wilderness period as a father carries a child (Deut 1:31).
63.10: But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit — so he turned against them as their enemy and himself fought against them. The first explicit reference to grieving the Holy Spirit in Scripture (Ephesians 4:30 echoes this directly: do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God). YHWH's response to grief — turning against them as their enemy — is the covenant-curse in its most personal form: the one who was their protector becomes their opponent. The divine warrior who fought for them now fights against them.
63.11: Then he remembered the ancient days, the time of Moses and his people. Where is the one who brought them up out of the sea with the shepherd of his flock? Where is the one who placed his Holy Spirit among them? The lament-question addresses YHWH's own apparent memory lapse: where is the God of the Exodus? The shepherd of the flock is Moses (or YHWH as Moses's principal); the Holy Spirit placed among the community is the divine presence that accompanied the wilderness community. The question implies: that God still exists — why has he not acted?
63.12: Who led Moses by the right hand with his glorious arm — dividing the waters before them to make an everlasting name for himself, who led them through the depths? Like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. Leading Moses by the right hand is the intimate guidance image: YHWH directly steering the leader as he steers a child learning to walk. The divided sea and the depths-crossing establish the Exodus as YHWH's name-making event — his reputation among the nations was built on this act. The horse-in-desert image captures confident, unimpeded movement in apparently impassable terrain.
63.13: Who led them through the depths — like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. As cattle descend into a valley to rest, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest. The Spirit as the agent of the Exodus rest-giving (the cloud by day and fire by night providing the community's orientation and rest) is now invoked as precedent for the Spirit's present activity. The cattle-descending-to-valley image is the comfortable, natural movement of domesticated animals toward their resting place: the Spirit made the wilderness feel like home.
63.14: As cattle descend into a valley to rest, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest — so you led your people to make yourself a glorious name. The Exodus series of Spirit-acts (dividing sea, leading through depths, giving rest) culminated in YHWH's name being made glorious among the nations. The lament implicitly asks: do the same name-making acts again — demonstrate your power now as you did then, so that your name is again glorified in the sight of the nations watching Israel's exile.
63.15: Look down from heaven and see, from your holy and glorious dwelling. Where are your zeal and your strength? The yearning of your heart and your compassion are withheld from me. The lament-invocation requesting divine attention from the heavenly throne room echoes Psalm 80:14. YHWH's zeal (qin'ah) — the passionate, jealous protection of covenant relationship — and his hamah (yearning of the bowels = visceral compassion) are appealed to directly. The complaint that these are withheld is not accusation but petition: the one who withholds has the power to release.
63.16: For you are our Father, though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us — you, LORD, are our Father; our Redeemer from of old, that is your name. The appeal to divine fatherhood over biological ancestry is remarkable: even if Abraham and Jacob (Israel) could not recognize the exilic community as their heirs, YHWH still owns the relationship as Father. Father is both intimate and covenantal — the term used by the returning prodigal and the title by which Israel addressed YHWH in covenant prayer (cf. Jer 3:19). Redeemer from of old grounds the appeal in YHWH's historical identity, not a new claim.
63.17: LORD, why do you let us stray from your ways and harden our hearts from fearing you? Turn back for the sake of your servants, the tribes of your inheritance. The theological boldness of the petition — asking YHWH why he permitted the apostasy that led to the exile — is the lament's most daring claim. The hardening of the heart is attributed to divine permission (cf. the hardening of Pharaoh's heart), not to autonomous human choice alone. Turning back (shuv) addresses both YHWH's return to the community and the community's return to YHWH — the two movements of covenant restoration are inseparable.
63.18: Your holy people possessed your sanctuary for a little while — now our adversaries have trampled it down. The brevity of the sanctuary's period of Israelite possession is the complaint: from Solomon's dedication (957 BCE) to the Babylonian destruction (586 BCE) was approximately 370 years. But measured against YHWH's eternal ownership of the holy mountain, this was brief. The trampling of the sanctuary by adversaries — the very place where YHWH's name dwelt — is the most acute form of covenant humiliation.
63.19: We have become like those you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name. The exile community's self-description as equivalent to the uncovenant pagans is the lament's sharpest formulation: the distinction between Israel and the nations has been erased by the catastrophe. Being called by YHWH's name (niqra shemkha alenu) was the covenant identity marker — to be like those who were never called by his name is to have lost the entire basis of covenant identity. The lament ends here, preparing for the theophany-prayer of chapter 64.

64.1: Oh, that you would tear open the heavens and come down — that the mountains would quake at your presence. The heaven-rending theophany request is the lament's climactic petition. In ANE cosmology, the heavens were a solid dome (raqia) separating the divine realm from the human; the request for YHWH to tear (qara) it open asks for a violent rupture of the normal cosmic boundary. Mountains quaking at the divine presence is the standard Sinai theophany vocabulary (Exod 19:16-18; Ps 114:4-7) — the exilic community wants Sinai-level divine intervention.
64.2: As when fire kindles brushwood and water boils — to make your name known to your enemies, so that the nations would tremble at your presence. The Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18: YHWH descended in fire; the smoke rose like smoke from a furnace) is the model. The nations trembling at the divine name repeats the Reed-Sea effect: at the Exodus, the nations heard and trembled (Exod 15:14-16). The petition asks for a repetition of name-making-by-theophany: demonstrate your power dramatically so that the nations fear you as at the Exodus.
64.3: When you did awe-inspiring things we did not expect, you came down — the mountains quaked at your presence. The historical precedent cited is the Sinai event and the wilderness theophanies. The phrase we did not expect (lo niqaveh) captures the quality of divine action that exceeds human anticipation: YHWH acted beyond what the community had calculated or hoped for. The appeal is: do the unexpected thing again — the thing no one dares to ask for specifically because it seems too large.
64.4: From ancient times no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. The uniqueness of YHWH as a God who acts for his waiting people distinguishes him from the ANE gods who required human action and ritual manipulation to secure their favor. YHWH's initiative-for-waiters is his distinctive covenantal character. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 2:9 (applied to the hidden wisdom now revealed by the Spirit) — but he applies it to what YHWH has prepared, extending the future horizon of the promise.
64.5: You come to the aid of those who joyfully do right, those who remember you in your ways. But you were angry, and we sinned — in our sins we have long continued, and how can we be saved? The transition from divine character to communal confession mirrors the structure of the Psalms of lament. The community acknowledges that YHWH supports the righteous but admits they are not that community: they sinned and continued in sin across generations. The rhetorical question — how can we be saved? — is not despair but the opening for divine answer: only by your unilateral action, since we have no merit to appeal to.
64.6: We have all become like one who is unclean — all our righteous deeds are like a filthy rag; we all wither like a leaf and our iniquities, like the wind, sweep us away. The filthy rag (begad iddim) is the menstrual cloth — the most extreme category of ritual impurity in Leviticus 15. Even the community's best religious performances are categorized at the most impure end of the purity-scale. The withering-leaf image captures transience and vulnerability: the wind sweeps both the autumn leaf and the community's sinful members away without resistance.
64.7: There is no one who calls on your name, who stirs himself to take hold of you — for you have hidden your face from us and given us over to the power of our iniquities. The absence of effective intercession (ein qore be-shimkha) is both diagnosis and the occasion for the prayer being offered: the community is praying precisely because no one has been praying. The hidden face (cf. 59:2) has been given a specific mechanism — YHWH gives them over (va-temugenu) to the power of their own iniquities, a self-reinforcing exile from the covenant relationship.
64.8: But now, LORD, you are our Father — we are the clay and you are our potter; we are all the work of your hands. The potter-clay image (drawn from Jeremiah 18:1-12) shifts the appeal from Israel's merit to YHWH's creative ownership: the clay has no rights before the potter, but the potter has invested himself in the clay's formation. The atah avinu (you are our Father) repeats the appeal of 63:16 — the Father-Potter relationship is the only ground of appeal when covenant merit is exhausted. Paul develops the potter-clay sovereignty in Romans 9:20-21.
64.9: Do not be exceedingly angry, LORD — do not remember iniquity forever. Please look — we are all your people. The three-part petition: limit your anger, forget the iniquity, see us as yours. The appeal to YHWH's self-limitation of anger echoes 40:1-2 (the comfort that speaks that Jerusalem's warfare is over, her iniquity paid). We are all your people is the covenant-identity claim made without merit: not we have behaved as your people but we are, by election, your people — the relationship predates and survives the failure.
64.10: Your holy cities have become a wasteland — Zion is a wilderness, Jerusalem a desolation. The lament catalogs the physical evidence of YHWH's absence: the holy cities (plural — all the Judean towns, not just Jerusalem) have become the midbar (wilderness/wasteland) that Israel was meant to have left at the Exodus. Zion and Jerusalem named specifically as wilderness and desolation are the visible proof of covenant disruption — the holy city has reverted to pre-creation formlessness.
64.11: Our holy and glorious house where our fathers praised you has been burned with fire, and all our cherished places have become desolate. The temple's destruction by Babylonian fire (2 Kgs 25:9) is the specific catastrophe the entire lament has been approaching. Our cherished places (machmadesenu) — the word from the root of the covenant's most desirable things — have become desolate. The temple was not merely a religious building but the intersection of heaven and earth, the cosmic axis of the covenant community's existence. Its burning is the most extreme form of the covenant's disruption.
64.12: Will you hold yourself back for all this, LORD? Will you keep silent while you afflict us so grievously? The lament's closing challenge-petition is the sharpest form of covenant prayer: given all this evidence of catastrophe, will YHWH continue to restrain himself and stay silent? The holding-back (titapaq) and silence (teheshah) are addressed directly to YHWH as choices he is making. The lament does not accept divine inaction as inevitable — it names it as a choice YHWH can change, and petitions for that change. The question hangs unanswered in the text, creating the expectation that chapter 65 will provide the divine response.

65.1: YHWH's response to the community lament (63:7–64:12) opens by reversing the complaint: it was not YHWH who was absent but Israel who was unreachable. The image of YHWH extending his hands and saying "Here I am! Here I am!" (hinnênî hinnênî) to a nation that did not ask is a reversal of the intercessory prayer posture — usually the petitioner cries "Here am I" to the divine call; here YHWH calls and Israel ignores. Paul reads vv.1-2 in Rom 10:20-21 as a textual warrant for the gentile mission — those who "did not ask" are the nations, and Israel is the "disobedient and contrary people." This is a typological inversion that Paul applies to his own experience of gentile receptivity versus Jewish resistance.
65.2: The image of YHWH stretching out his hands (pēraśtî yādāy) throughout the day (kol-hayyôm) to a "rebellious people" (ʿam sôrēr) is drawn from the parental posture of patient outstretched welcome. Deut 21:18-21 uses sôrēr for the rebellious son who is to be brought before the elders; YHWH is the parent whose child has become the "rebellious son." The people "walk in the way that is not good" (hahōlĕkîm hadderek lōʾ-ṭôḇ) — the "two ways" tradition of Psalm 1 and Deut 30:15-20 frames the contrast.
65.3: The specific indictments of vv.3-5 describe practices of popular religion that competed with orthodox Yahwism in the late exilic and early post-exilic period. "Sacrificing in gardens" (haššōḥăṭîm baggannôt) refers to outdoor shrines associated with fertility cults, possibly connected to Asherah worship. "Burning incense on bricks" (ûmĕqaṭṭĕrîm ʿal-halĕbēnîm) — the specific mention of bricks (a Babylonian building material) may allude to practices borrowed during the exile. The practices represent the syncretism that post-exilic prophets like Malachi and Zechariah also confronted.
65.4: "Sitting in tombs" (hayyōšĕḇîm baqqĕḇārîm) and spending nights in hidden places (ûḇannĕṣûrîm yālînû) are necromantic practices — consulting the dead by sleeping at their graves to receive revelatory dreams. The same practice is condemned in 8:19-20 ("should not a people consult their God? Should they consult the dead on behalf of the living?"). "Eating pig's flesh" (ʾōkĕlê bĕśar haḥăzîr) violates Lev 11:7-8; Deut 14:8 — a marker of gentile practice adopted by some Israelites. "Broth of unclean things in their vessels" (ûmĕraq piggulîm kĕlêhem) — piggûl is a technical term for sacrificial meat that has been kept past its lawful time (Lev 7:18; 19:7).
65.5: "Keep to yourself, do not come near me, for I am holy to you" (qĕḏaštîkā) — this is a parody of the Holiness Code's language. In Lev 19:2 and Num 16:5 YHWH declares his own holiness or the holiness of those he chooses; here the idolaters appropriate the holiness-language to exclude others from their rituals, as if their forbidden practices had made them more sacred. The irony is pointed: those who defile themselves most thoroughly claim the greatest purity. The phrase echoes the false prophets condemned in Mic 3:5 and Jer 6:13-14 who cry "peace" when there is no peace.
65.6: The divine record-keeping: "behold, it is written before me" (hinnēh kĕtûḇâ lĕpānāy) — the image of a heavenly register where sins are documented awaiting judgment. The phrase "I will not keep silent but I will repay" (kî ʾim-šillmtî wĕlōʾ ʾeḥĕšeh) is particularly significant as it answers the community's complaint in 64:12: "Will you keep silent?" YHWH was not silent; he was recording. The repayment will be "into their bosom" (el-ḥêqām) — the breast/bosom was the place where one gathered grain or carried provisions; paying into the bosom means settling the account in full.
65.7: The intergenerational accounting: "your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities together" (ʿăwōnōtêkem wĕʿăwōnōt ʾăḇôtêkem yaḥdāw). The burning incense "on the mountains" (ʿal-hehārîm) and "reviling me on the hills" (ḥērĕpûnî ʿal-haggĕḇāʿôt) describes the high place worship condemned in Hosea 4:13; 2 Kings 17:9-11. The ancestral sin framework does not eliminate individual responsibility but situates it within a generational pattern — the post-exilic community is not starting fresh but continuing a history that must be acknowledged and discontinued.
65.8: The grape-cluster parable: "as new wine is found in a cluster" (kaʾăšer yimmāṣēʾ hatîrôš bĕʾeškôl) — the good wine discovered within an otherwise spoiled cluster. The instruction "do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it" (ʾal tašḥitēhû kî bĕrākâ bô) parallels the decision to spare the remnant from within a largely corrupt community. The grape-cluster image connects to the vineyard parable of ch. 5 (where the vineyard failed to produce good grapes) — now within the failed cluster, YHWH finds the faithful remnant worth preserving.
65.9: "I will bring out from Jacob a seed/offspring" (wĕhôṣĕʾtî miyyaʿăqōb zeraʿ) — the remnant as seed (cf. 1:9 "unless YHWH of hosts had left us a very small remnant, we should have been like Sodom"; 6:13 "the holy seed is its stump"). The promise of possessing the mountain (wĕyārĕšâ ʿam hārî) echoes the original conquest language — the return from exile as a new entering-the-land. "My chosen ones shall inhabit it" (ûbĕḥîray yišĕkĕnû-šām) — the election category (bāḥar) that defines the Servant (42:1) now extends to the remnant community.
65.10: The specific geography of restoration — Sharon (haššārôn), the coastal plain of central Israel, and the Valley of Achor (ʿēmeq ʿākôr) — provides historical concreteness to the eschatological promises. The Valley of Achor was the place of Achan's sin and execution (Josh 7:24-26) — a place of cursed memory. Hosea 2:15 had already promised that the Valley of Achor would become "a door of hope" for the restored remnant. Isaiah here echoes and deepens that promise: the most cursed geography becomes pastoral land for YHWH's seeking people.
65.11: The fate-deity worship condemned here identifies the specific religious competition in the post-exilic community. "Gad" (gāḏ, H1408) is a deity of fortune or fate known from personal names in the ancient Near East; "Meni" (mĕnî, H4507) is the deity of destiny (possibly related to the word for "portion" or "allotment"). Setting a table for these deities and filling cups of drink-offering for them represents the practice of honoring the deities who control fate — a temptation especially acute in a community of returnees whose survival felt contingent on cosmic powers. YHWH responds: those who chose Fate will receive judgment as their portion.
65.12: The wordplay on "Destiny" (mĕnî) and "I will destine/appoint" (mānîtî): you chose the deity of Destiny, so YHWH himself will appoint your destiny — to the sword. The logic reverses the promise: those who worshipped the god of fate receive YHWH's direct assignment of their fate. "You did not answer when I called, you did not listen when I spoke" (yaʿan qārāʾtî wĕlōʾ ʿănittem dibbarti wĕlōʾ šĕmaʿtem) — the accusation of non-response echoes 50:2 and 66:4, framing the entire end of Isaiah around the theme of YHWH's call and Israel's refusal.
65.13: The double-destiny discourse of vv.13-16 is structured as a series of antitheses: "my servants shall eat, but you shall be hungry; my servants shall drink, but you shall be thirsty; my servants shall rejoice, but you shall be ashamed." The same history (exile, return, restoration) produces opposite outcomes for those who trusted YHWH and those who did not. This structure anticipates the judgment parables of Jesus in which the same master, the same banquet, the same wedding produces inclusion for some and exclusion for others (Matt 25:1-13; Luke 14:15-24).
65.14: "My servants shall sing for joy of heart" (yārōnnû miṭṭûḇ-lēḇ) contrasted with "you shall cry out for pain of heart" (tizʿăqû mimmakʾôḇ-lēḇ). The heart (lēḇ) is the center of the antithesis — the same organ that rejoices or grieves. "From anguish of spirit you shall wail" (ûmišĕḇer rûaḥ tiylîlû) — the šĕḇer (brokenness) of the spirit echoes the "broken spirit" that YHWH does not despise in Ps 51:17; here the spirit's brokenness is the consequence of rebellion rather than its cure.
65.15: "Your name shall be left for my chosen ones as a curse" (ûhinnãḥtem šĕmĕkem libḥîray lišĕḇûʿâ) — the proper name of the rebellious community becomes the formula of cursing ("YHWH make you like those"), in contrast to the Abrahamic promise in Gen 12:2 ("I will make your name great" as a formula of blessing). "But his servants he will call by another name" — the new name promises covenant renewal; cf. 56:5 (the eunuch's everlasting name) and 62:2 (a new name for Jerusalem).
65.16: The double formula "whoever blesses himself in the land shall bless himself by the God of truth (ʾĕlōhê ʾāmēn)" — the Hebrew ʾāmēn used as a divine title ("God of Amen/Truth") is unique. This becomes a significant motif in the NT where Jesus begins statements with "Amen, amen I say to you" — the Amen is a divine truth-claim. "The former troubles are forgotten and hidden from my eyes" (kî niškĕḥû haṣṣārôt hārîʾšōnôt wĕkî nistarrû mēʿênāy) — the forgetting of former distress provides the theological basis for the new creation oracle that follows.
65.17: "For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth" (hinnî-ḇōrēʾ šāmayim ḥăḏāšîm wĕʾāreṣ ḥăḏāšâ) — the verb bārāʾ (to create) is used exclusively of divine creation in the Hebrew Bible and is the same verb as Gen 1:1. The new creation is not renovation but a creative act of the same order as the original. "The former things shall not be remembered or come to mind" (wĕlōʾ tizzākarenâ hārîʾšōnôt wĕlōʾ taʿălênâ ʿal-lēḇ) — the forgetting of the former world is not amnesia but the eclipse of the old by the overwhelming goodness of the new. Revelation 21:1 ("then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away") and 2 Pet 3:13 ("we are waiting for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells") both draw on this verse.
65.18: "But be glad and rejoice forever in what I create" (kî ʾim-śîśû wĕgîlû ʿăḏê-ʿaḏ ʾăšer ʾănî bôrēʾ) — the new creation is designed for perpetual celebration. "Behold, I create Jerusalem to be a joy and her people to be a gladness" — the recreated Jerusalem is defined by what it produces: joy and gladness. The city is not rebuilt but recreated (bôrēʾ), which means its fundamental character is changed, not merely its condition improved. The specific naming of Jerusalem within the cosmic new creation anchors the universal promise in a particular covenantal location.
65.19: "I will rejoice in Jerusalem and be glad in my people" (wĕgaltî bîrûšālaim wĕśaśtî bĕʿammî) — the divine joy complements the human joy of v.18. The mutual joy — YHWH in his people and people in YHWH — is the relational content of the new creation. "The sound of weeping shall be heard no more in it, nor the cry of distress" — the absence of the sounds of mourning defines the new creation negatively. This verse is the immediate background for Rev 21:4 ("he will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain").
65.20: The new creation's altered relationship to death is not the full abolition of death (that appears in 25:8 and Rev 21:4) but a radical extension of life: a person dying at a hundred years old will be considered a youth, and a hundred-year-old sinner will be accounted as one cut off. This intermediate vision — greatly extended life rather than immortality — represents a stage in the eschatological imagination that is developed further in Rev 20:4-6 (the thousand-year reign) before the fully deathless new creation of Rev 21.
65.21: "They shall build houses and inhabit them; they shall plant vineyards and eat their fruit" — the exact reversal of the Deuteronomy 28:30 covenant curse: "You shall build a house, but you shall not dwell in it. You shall plant a vineyard, but you shall not enjoy its fruit." The new creation is characterized structurally by the removal of the covenant curses. The Holiness Code's blessings (Lev 26:3-13) and curses (26:14-39) have established the framework; the new creation represents the permanent installation of the blessing structure and the permanent elimination of the curse structure.
65.22: "They shall not build and another inhabit; they shall not plant and another eat" — the covenant security is expressed through the stable connection between labor and fruit. The simile "for like the days of a tree shall the days of my people be" (kî kimê haʿēṣ yĕmê ʿammî) — the longevity of the great trees (cedar, oak) is the measure of the new creation's lifespan. "My chosen ones shall long enjoy the work of their hands" (ûmiḇḥîray yĕballû maʿăśēh yĕḏêhem) — the election theme that framed the remnant theology (vv.9,15) culminates here in the enjoyment of one's own labor as the specific content of election's blessing.
65.23: "They shall not labor in vain or bear children for calamity" (lōʾ yîgĕʿû lāreiq wĕlōʾ yēlĕdû labĕhālâ) — another reversal of the covenant curses (Deut 28:41: "you shall father sons and daughters, but they shall not be yours"). The promise extends to intergenerational blessing: "they and their offspring with them" (hēmmâ wĕzarʿām ʾittām). The "blessed of YHWH" (zereʿ bĕrûkê Yhwh) identity is inheritable — the new covenant blessing flows through generations as the original Abrahamic blessing was designed to do.
65.24: "Before they call I will answer; while they are yet speaking I will hear" (wĕhāyâ ṭerem-yiqrĕʾû wĕʾănî ʾeʿĕneh ʿôd hēm mĕḏabbĕrîm wĕʾănî ʾešmaʿ) — the new creation's relational register: no more of the silence complaint (63:15; 64:12) or the "I was present and they did not answer" (65:12). The proactive divine responsiveness — answering before the request is complete — represents the fullness of covenantal intimacy. John 16:23-24 ("ask and you will receive") and 1 John 5:14-15 (confidence that God hears) develop this promise further.
65.25: The return of Eden's harmony between creatures: "the wolf and the lamb shall graze together, the lion shall eat straw like the ox" (zĕʾēḇ wĕṭāleh yirʿû yāḥaḏ wĕʾaryēh kattābên yōʾkal) — identical motifs to 11:6-9 (the peaceable kingdom), where the wolf lies with the lamb and the lion eats straw. The repetition confirms that the new creation and the messianic kingdom are facets of the same eschatological reality in Isaiah's vision. "And dust shall be the serpent's food" (wĕnāḥāš ʿāpār laḥmô) — the serpent's curse from Gen 3:14 ("on your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat") persists into the new creation. The serpent's curse is not reversed but acknowledged — it remains as the permanent memorial of the judgment that was escaped, the one element from the old order that marks the new.

66.1: The claim that heaven is YHWH's throne and earth his footstool (hašāmayim kissî wĕhā'āreṣ hădom raglāy) directly challenges the ideology of temple-as-divine-dwelling. Solomon acknowledged this tension at the temple's dedication (1 Kgs 8:27: will God indeed dwell on the earth?). The ANE background assumed temples housed deities — Israel's view was always more complex, holding divine transcendence alongside cultic presence. Stephen quotes v. 1-2 in Acts 7:49-50 to argue that the Jerusalem temple does not contain God.
66.2: YHWH made all things (yādî 'āśāt kol-'ēlleh) — creator transcendence is the ground for temple critique. The one YHWH regards is the 'ānî ûnĕkēh-rûaḥ ûḥārēd 'al-dĕb̠ārî — the afflicted and contrite of spirit who trembles at his word. In Second Temple Judaism, the ḥărēdîm (trembling ones, cf. v. 5) became a recognized category of the faithful remnant who maintained strict covenantal seriousness. This concept is proto-typical of the pietist streams within post-exilic Judaism.
66.3: The verse pairs legitimate sacrifices with abhorrent acts in a catalog of syncretism: slaughtering an ox AND killing a man (human sacrifice); offering a lamb AND breaking a dog's neck (ritually unclean animal); grain offering AND pig's blood; frankincense AND worshipping idols. This is not either/or but both/and — the practices are simultaneous. The post-exilic context included continued pressure to assimilate to surrounding cult practices. Ezekiel 8:16-18 describes similar syncretistic abominations practiced within the temple precincts.
66.4: YHWH will choose their delusions (tĕ'alûlêhem — capricious things/terrors; the term appears only here and in Ps 119:37) as a form of judicial irony: those who chose their own ways will receive those ways fully enacted upon them. This is the theological principle of divine abandonment to the consequences of one's choices (cf. Rom 1:24,26,28 where Paul uses the same logic of YHWH giving over those who suppress truth).
66.5: The ḥărēdîm — those who tremble at YHWH's word — are a distinct community within Israel, addressed separately from the apostates. Their opponents speak mockingly of YHWH's glory (yikabed YHWH) — whether this is genuine religious claim or sarcasm aimed at the trembling ones is debated. The situation reflects internal communal conflict in post-exilic Judah between strict Torah-observers (the returnees' party) and those who had accommodated to Babylonian or Persian religious culture. The same conflict surfaces in Ezra 9-10, Neh 13, and Mal 2-3.
66.6: The sudden divine voice from the city and temple (qôl šā'ôn mē'îr qôl mēhêkāl qôl YHWH mĕšallēm gĕmûl) is in the tradition of the divine warrior theophany — a battle roar announcing judgment. Compare Amos 1:2 (YHWH roars from Zion) and Joel 3:16 (YHWH roars from Jerusalem). The temple is here the launching point of divine judgment rather than a refuge from it — reversal of the popular Zion theology that treated the temple's presence as automatic protection.
66.7: The birth-before-labor imagery (b̠eṭerem tāḥîl yāl ĕdāh bĕṭerem yāb̠ô' ḥēbel lāh) reverses the normal sequence: the baby arrives before the birth pangs begin. ANE mythology and royal ideology frequently used birth metaphors to describe the creation of cities, nations, and dynasties — the maternity of cities and their divine patrons was a common image in Mesopotamian literature. Israel used birth language for the exodus (the birth of the nation) and here for the eschatological restoration.
66.8: The rhetorical question — mî-šāma' k̠āzō't (who has heard such a thing?) — acknowledges that what YHWH is about to do is unprecedented. The birth of a nation in a day (hăyûkāl 'èreṣ bĕyôm 'eḥād) recalls the speed of the exodus: Israel moved from slavery to nation-status virtually overnight. The post-exilic audience would have read this against the backdrop of their slow, difficult return — YHWH promises a different kind of restoration: sudden, complete, and undeniable.
66.9: The midwifery metaphor: hă'ănî 'aśbîr wĕlō' 'ôlîd — shall I open the womb and not cause birth? In ANE thought, the goddess Nintu (or Ishtar in Babylonian tradition) served as divine midwife at births of importance. YHWH here takes the role of the divine midwife — he who opens the womb (cf. Gen 30:22; 1 Sam 1:5) will not abandon what he has begun. The theological principle: divine initiation guarantees divine completion.
66.10: The call to rejoice with Jerusalem (śimĕḥû 'et-yĕrûšālaim) presupposes a congregation gathered around the city — the 'ōhăb̠êhā (those who love her) are pilgrims and worshippers whose identity is bound to Zion. Psalm 122 uses exactly this language: pray for the peace of Jerusalem; may those who love you prosper. The Zion psalms (46, 48, 76, 84, 87, 122) form the liturgical background for this call — community identity organized around the holy city.
66.11: The maternal imagery of Zion nursing her children with abundance (mišad tanḥumêhā — from the breast of her consolations) draws on the ancient Near Eastern goddess traditions of the nurturing city-mother. In Mesopotamian lament literature, the destruction of a city was depicted as a mother losing her children; the restoration was depicted as the mother receiving them back. Lamentations uses similar imagery (1:2: all her friends betrayed her; cf. 66:13 where YHWH himself takes the mother's role).
66.12: The river of peace (nāhār šālôm) and overflowing torrent of national wealth (naḥal šōṭēp̄ — a rushing wadi in flood) draw on the ANE cosmic river traditions. Babylon was built on rivers (Ps 137:1); Egypt was sustained by the Nile. The eschatological river flowing to Jerusalem (Ezek 47; Zech 14:8) represents the reversal of desert-barrenness: the cosmic life-giving river redirected to Zion. Revelation 22:1-2 presents the river of life flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb.
66.13: The explicit maternal simile for YHWH (kĕ'îš 'ăšer 'immô tĕnaḥămennû — as a man whose mother comforts him) is one of the most striking feminine divine images in the Hebrew Bible. Isa 49:15 uses the same comparison (can a nursing mother forget her child?). This does not feminize YHWH but uses the most universally recognized image of intimate consoling love — the mother-child bond — to communicate the intensity of divine comfort. The divine name used here is YHWH, the covenantal name, grounding the maternal metaphor in covenant faithfulness.
66.14: The flourishing of bones like grass (wĕ'aṣmôtêk̠em k̠addeše' tipraḥnāh) echoes Ezek 37 (dry bones restored to life) and the restoration of physical vitality. The recognition formula: wĕnôda' yad YHWH 'et-'ăb̠ādāyw — the hand of YHWH will be made known to his servants, and his indignation to his enemies. The contrast between the fate of servants and enemies is a recurring structure in the closing chapters of Isaiah (cf. 65:8-16).
66.15: The fiery theophany (kî-hinnēh YHWH b̠ā'ēš yāb̠ô') draws on the tradition of YHWH's appearances in fire: the burning bush (Exod 3:2), Sinai (Exod 19:18), the pillar of fire (Exod 13:21), and the theophanic fire in 1 Kings 18 (Elijah on Carmel). Chariots (markāb̠ôt) are the standard equipment of the divine warrior; in Canaanite mythology, Baal rides the clouds in his chariot. YHWH's chariots here are instruments of judgment rather than storm-force.
66.16: The cosmic judgment by fire and sword echoes the divine-warrior oracle tradition: YHWH executes judgment (nišpāṭ, Niphal — judgment is done to the earth) against all flesh simultaneously. The ḥallĕlê YHWH (the slain of YHWH) recalls the aftermath of holy war in texts like Jer 25:33. This is judgment of universal scope — not limited to one nation but directed at all who rebel.
66.17: The garden cult sites (miqqaddĕšîm ûmittaharîm 'el-haggannôt — those who sanctify and purify themselves for the gardens) recall Isa 65:3 and the Canaanite garden-sanctuary practices. The forbidden foods — pig (hazzîr), mouse (hā'ak̠b̠ār), and detestable things (haššèqeṣ) — are specifically enumerated in the Levitical purity code (Lev 11:7, 11:29). Consuming them as part of a sacrificial ritual represents a complete inversion of covenantal identity: the boundary markers that define Israel as YHWH's people are destroyed.
66.18: YHWH's omniscience over deeds and thoughts (yāda'tî ma'ăśêhem wĕmaḥšĕb̠ōtêhem) grounds the gathering of all nations and tongues for the revelation of his glory. The phrase wĕrā'û 'et-kĕb̠ôdî — they shall see my glory — echoes the theophanic disclosure of YHWH's k̠āb̠ôd (weighty presence) at Sinai (Exod 24:16-17), in Isaiah's vision (ch. 6), and Ezekiel's chariot-vision. The eschatological gathering is a universal Sinai event.
66.19: The survivors (pĕlêṭîm — those who escape) are sent as missionaries to distant nations: Tarshish (western Mediterranean, possibly Spain), Pul and Lud (Africa — possibly Punt and Libya), Tubal (Black Sea region, Asia Minor), and Javan (יָוָן — Greece, specifically the Ionian Greeks). From Israel's perspective these represented the geographical edges of the known world. The mission: wĕhiggîdû 'et-kĕb̠ôdî baggôyim — to declare my glory among the nations. This is the first explicit universal mission mandate in the Hebrew Bible, anticipating the Apostolic mission of Acts.
66.20: The nations function as carriers of diaspora Jews back to Jerusalem — on horses, chariots, litters (ṣāb̠îm — covered carriages), mules, and swift camels. This reverses the exile imagery: as Israel was taken into captivity by foreign powers, so the foreign powers now bring Israel home. The diaspora Jews are brought as a minḥāh (grain offering) to YHWH — priestly cultic language applied to a human gathering, framing the return as an act of worship.
66.21: The most radical eschatological claim of the chapter: wĕgam-mēhem 'eqqaḥ lakkōhănîm lallĕwiyyim — from the Gentile nations, YHWH will take some as priests and Levites. The Levitical priesthood was strictly hereditary — only sons of Aaron could serve as priests (Num 18:1-7). This verse breaks the hereditary principle entirely for the eschatological age. Second Temple Judaism debated whether Gentiles could be incorporated into the covenant community; the NT letters of Paul engage precisely this question (Gal 3:28; Eph 2:11-22).
66.22: The new heavens and new earth (hašāmayim haḥădāšîm wĕhā'āreṣ haḥădāšāh, cf. Isa 65:17) provide the measure of permanence for Zion's seed and name: as permanent as the new cosmic order. The phrase appears in Isa 65:17; 66:22; and is taken up in 2 Pet 3:13 and Rev 21:1. In Jewish eschatology, the current age ('ôlām hazzeh) will give way to the coming age ('ôlām hab̠bā'); the new creation is the coming age's cosmic framework.
66.23: The new-moon and Sabbath calendar (mîddē-ḥōdèš bĕḥoḏšô ûmiddē-šabbāt bĕšabbattô) frames universal worship within Israel's existing liturgical calendar — the eschatological worship is not a break from Israel's cult but its universal extension. kol-b̠āśār — all flesh — will come to worship, the same phrase used in v. 16 for those subject to divine judgment. The same humanity that faced divine fire now participates in perpetual worship. The calendar is democratized and universalized.
66.24: The final verse is deliberately jarring: those who go out from universal worship will see the corpses of rebels against YHWH (bĕpigrê hā'ănāšîm happ̄ōšĕ'îm b̠î). The worm and fire that do not cease (tolĕ'ātām lō' tāmût wĕ'iššām lō' tik̠beh) describe the aftermath of conquest — bodies left unburied. The Valley of Hinnom (Hebrew: gê hinnōm, the source of the Greek Geenna/Gehenna) outside Jerusalem served in the Second Temple period as a burning garbage dump — the perpetually-smoldering site associated with judgment. Jesus quotes this verse three times in Mark 9:44, 46, 48 to describe the fate of the unrighteous. The book of Isaiah closes not with universal triumph alone but with the permanent visibility of judgment — a warning inseparable from the promise.
